title: Scaling Ultrasound Volumetric Reconstruction via Mobile Augmented Reality
summary: Accurate volumetric characterization of lesions is essential for oncologic diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment planning. While imaging modalities such as Computed Tomography provide high-quality 3D data, 2D ultrasound (2D-US) remains the preferred first-line modality for breast and thyroid imaging due to cost, portability, and safety factors. However, volume estimates derived from 2D-US suffer from high inter-user variability even among experienced clinicians. Existing 3D ultrasound (3D-US) solutions use specialized probes or external tracking hardware, but such configurations increase costs and diminish portability, constraining widespread clinical use. To address these limitations, we present Mobile Augmented Reality Volumetric Ultrasound (MARVUS), a resource-efficient system designed to increase accessibility to accurate and reproducible volumetric assessment. MARVUS is interoperable with conventional ultrasound (US) systems, using a foundation model to enhance cross-specialty generalization while minimizing hardware requirements relative to current 3D-US solutions. In a user study involving experienced clinicians performing measurements on breast phantoms, MARVUS yielded a substantial improvement in volume estimation accuracy (mean difference: 0.469 cm3) with reduced inter-user variability (mean difference: 0.417 cm3). Additionally, we prove that augmented reality (AR) visualizations enhance objective performance metrics and clinician-reported usability. Collectively, our findings suggests that MARVUS can enhance US-based cancer screening, diagnostic workflows, and treatment planning in a scalable, cost-conscious, and resource-efficient manner. Usage video demonstration available (https://youtu.be/m4llYcZpqmM).

AI.tldr: MARVUS is a resource-efficient mobile augmented reality system that enhances volumetric ultrasound measurements by integrating AR visualizations with a foundation model, improving accuracy and reducing inter-user variability compared to conventional 2D ultrasound methods.
AI.motivation: 2D ultrasound is preferred for breast and thyroid imaging due to cost, portability, and safety, but volume estimates suffer from high inter-user variability. Existing 3D ultrasound solutions require specialized probes or external tracking hardware, increasing costs and diminishing portability, which constrains widespread clinical adoption.
AI.method: MARVUS interoperates with conventional ultrasound systems and uses a foundation model to enhance cross-specialty generalization while minimizing hardware requirements. The system employs augmented reality visualizations during volumetric measurements, and was validated through a user study with experienced clinicians performing measurements on breast phantoms.
AI.result: In the user study, MARVUS yielded a substantial improvement in volume estimation accuracy (mean difference: 0.469 cm³) and reduced inter-user variability (mean difference: 0.417 cm³) compared to conventional methods. AR visualizations were shown to enhance both objective performance metrics and clinician-reported usability.
AI.conclusion: MARVUS demonstrates that accurate and reproducible volumetric assessment can be achieved in a scalable, cost-conscious, and resource-efficient manner, potentially enhancing US-based cancer screening, diagnostic workflows, and treatment planning.

title: Feedback-based Automated Verification in Vibe Coding of CAS Adaptation Built on Constraint Logic
summary:
In CAS adaptation, a challenge is to define the dynamic architecture of the system and changes in its behavior. Implementation-wise, this is projected into an adaptation mechanism, typically realized as an Adaptation Manager (AM). With the advances of generative LLMs, generating AM code based on system specification and desired AM behavior (partially in natural language) is a tempting opportunity. The recent introduction of vibe coding suggests a way to target the problem of the correctness of generated code by iterative testing and vibe coding feedback loops instead of direct code inspection.
  In this paper, we show that generating an AM via vibe coding feedback loops is a viable option when the verification of the generated AM is based on a very precise formulation of the functional requirements. We specify these as constraints in a novel temporal logic FCL that allows us to express the behavior of traces with much finer granularity than classical LTL enables.
  Furthermore, we show that by combining the adaptation and vibe coding feedback loops where the FCL constraints are evaluated for the current system state, we achieved good results in the experiments with generating AMs for two example systems from the CAS domain. Typically, just a few feedback loop iterations were necessary, each feeding the LLM with reports describing detailed violations of the constraints. This AM testing was combined with high run path coverage achieved by different initial settings.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using vibe coding feedback loops with a novel temporal logic FCL to generate Adaptation Manager code for CAS adaptation, showing it's viable when verification is based on precise functional requirements.
AI.motivation: The challenge in CAS adaptation is defining dynamic architecture and behavior changes. With advances in generative LLMs, generating AM code based on specifications is an opportunity, but ensuring correctness through direct code inspection is difficult. Vibe coding offers a way to target code correctness through iterative testing and feedback loops.
AI.method: The authors generate AMs via vibe coding feedback loops, where verification is based on precise functional requirements specified as constraints in a novel temporal logic called FCL that allows expressing behavior of traces with finer granularity than classical LTL. The approach combines adaptation and vibe coding feedback loops where FCL constraints are evaluated for the current system state.
AI.result: Experiments with two CAS example systems showed good results. Typically, just a few feedback loop iterations were necessary, each feeding the LLM with reports describing detailed violations of the constraints. This AM testing was combined with high run path coverage achieved by different initial settings.
AI.conclusion: Generating AMs using vibe coding feedback loops with precise FCL constraints is a viable option for CAS adaptation, providing an effective approach to ensure correctness when verification is based on very precise functional requirements.

title: Decentralized Attention Fails Centralized Signals: Rethinking Transformers for Medical Time Series
comment: Accepted by ICLR 2026 (Oral). arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2405.19363 by other authors
summary: Accurate analysis of medical time series (MedTS) data, such as electroencephalography (EEG) and electrocardiography (ECG), plays a pivotal role in healthcare applications, including the diagnosis of brain and heart diseases. MedTS data typically exhibit two critical patterns: temporal dependencies within individual channels and channel dependencies across multiple channels. While recent advances in deep learning have leveraged Transformer-based models to effectively capture temporal dependencies, they often struggle with modeling channel dependencies. This limitation stems from a structural mismatch: MedTS signals are inherently centralized, whereas the Transformer's attention mechanism is decentralized, making it less effective at capturing global synchronization and unified waveform patterns. To address this mismatch, we propose CoTAR (Core Token Aggregation-Redistribution), a centralized MLP-based module designed to replace decentralized attention. Instead of allowing all tokens to interact directly, as in standard attention, CoTAR introduces a global core token that serves as a proxy to facilitate inter-token interactions, thereby enforcing a centralized aggregation and redistribution strategy. This design not only better aligns with the centralized nature of MedTS signals but also reduces computational complexity from quadratic to linear. Experiments on five benchmarks validate the superiority of our method in both effectiveness and efficiency, achieving up to a 12.13% improvement on the APAVA dataset, while using only 33% of the memory and 20% of the inference time compared to the previous state of the art. Code and all training scripts are available at https://github.com/Levi-Ackman/TeCh.

AI.tldr: CoTAR proposes a centralized MLP-based module with a global core token to replace decentralized attention in Transformer models for medical time series analysis, improving both effectiveness and efficiency by reducing computational complexity from quadratic to linear.
AI.motivation: Medical time series (MedTS) data like EEG and ECG exhibit two critical patterns: temporal dependencies within individual channels and channel dependencies across multiple channels. Recent Transformer-based models struggle with modeling channel dependencies due to a structural mismatch - MedTS signals are inherently centralized while the Transformer's attention mechanism is decentralized, making it less effective at capturing global synchronization and unified waveform patterns.
AI.method: CoTAR (Core Token Aggregation-Redistribution) introduces a centralized MLP-based module that replaces decentralized attention by introducing a global core token as a proxy to facilitate inter-token interactions. This design enforces a centralized aggregation and redistribution strategy, reducing computational complexity from quadratic to linear.
AI.result: Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate CoTAR's superiority in both effectiveness and efficiency. It achieved up to 12.13% improvement on the APAVA dataset while using only 33% of the memory and 20% of the inference time compared to the previous state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: CoTAR's design better aligns with the centralized nature of MedTS signals, successfully addressing the structural mismatch problem. The method validates its superiority through comprehensive experiments, offering a more efficient and effective approach for medical time series analysis.

title: EDU-MATRIX: A Society-Centric Generative Cognitive Digital Twin Architecture for Secondary Education
summary: Existing multi-agent simulations often suffer from the "Agent-Centric Paradox": rules are hard-coded into individual agents, making complex social dynamics rigid and difficult to align with educational values. This paper presents EDU-MATRIX, a society-centric generative cognitive digital twin architecture that shifts the paradigm from simulating "people" to simulating a "social space with a gravitational field." We introduce three architectural contributions: (1) An Environment Context Injection Engine (ECIE), which acts as a "social microkernel," dynamically injecting institutional rules (Gravity) into agents based on their spatial-temporal coordinates; (2) A Modular Logic Evolution Protocol (MLEP), where knowledge exists as "fluid" capsules that agents synthesize to generate new paradigms, ensuring high dialogue consistency (94.1%); and (3) Endogenous Alignment via Role-Topology, where safety constraints emerge from the agent's position in the social graph rather than external filters. Deployed as a digital twin of a secondary school with 2,400 agents, the system demonstrates how "social gravity" (rules) and "cognitive fluids" (knowledge) interact to produce emergent, value-aligned behaviors (Social Clustering Coefficient: 0.72).

AI.tldr: EDU-MATRIX is a society-centric generative cognitive digital twin architecture that replaces hard-coded agent rules with a dynamic "social space with gravitational field" approach, demonstrating value-aligned emergent behaviors in a 2,400-agent secondary school simulation.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent simulations suffer from the "Agent-Centric Paradox" where rules are hard-coded into individual agents, making complex social dynamics rigid and difficult to align with educational values.
AI.method: Three architectural innovations: (1) Environment Context Injection Engine (ECIE) as a "social microkernel" that dynamically injects institutional rules (Gravity) into agents based on spatial-temporal coordinates; (2) Modular Logic Evolution Protocol (MLEP) using "fluid" knowledge capsules that agents synthesize to ensure high dialogue consistency (94.1%); (3) Endogenous Alignment via Role-Topology where safety constraints emerge from agent positions in the social graph.
AI.result: Deployed as a digital twin of a secondary school with 2,400 agents, the system demonstrates how "social gravity" and "cognitive fluids" interact to produce emergent, value-aligned behaviors, achieving a Social Clustering Coefficient of 0.72.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from agent-centric to society-centric modeling enables more flexible, value-aligned simulations of complex social dynamics by allowing rules and safety constraints to emerge naturally from the social structure rather than being hard-coded into individual agents.

title: Spherical Hermite Maps
summary: Spherical functions appear throughout computer graphics, from spherical harmonic lighting and precomputed radiance transfer to neural radiance fields and procedural planet rendering. Efficient evaluation is critical for real-time applications, yet existing approaches face a quality-performance trade-off: bilinear LUT sampling is fast but produces faceting, while bicubic filtering requires 16 texture samples. Most implementations use finite differences for normals, requiring extra samples and introducing noise. This paper presents Spherical Hermite Maps, a derivative-augmented LUT representation that resolves this trade-off. By storing function values alongside scaled partial derivatives at each texel of a padded cubemap, bicubic-Hermite reconstruction is enabled from only four texture samples (a 2x2 footprint) while providing continuous gradients from the same samples. The key insight is that Hermite interpolation reconstructs smooth derivatives as a byproduct of value reconstruction, making surface normals effectively free. In controlled experiments, Spherical Hermite Maps improve PSNR by 8-41 dB over bilinear interpolation and match 16-tap bicubic quality at one-quarter the cost. Analytic normals reduce mean angular error by 9-13% on complex surfaces while yielding stable specular highlights. Three applications demonstrate versatility: spherical harmonic glyph visualization, radial depth-map impostors for mesh level-of-detail, and procedural planet/asteroid rendering with spherical heightfields.

AI.tldr: Spherical Hermite Maps introduces a derivative-augmented LUT representation that enables bicubic-quality spherical function interpolation from only 4 texture samples (2x2 footprint) while providing continuous gradients for free, eliminating the quality-performance trade-off of existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Spherical functions are widely used in computer graphics (spherical harmonic lighting, precomputed radiance transfer, neural radiance fields, procedural planet rendering). Existing approaches face a quality-performance trade-off: bilinear LUT sampling is fast but produces faceting, while bicubic filtering requires 16 texture samples. Additionally, finite differences for normals require extra samples and introduce noise.
AI.method: Spherical Hermite Maps stores function values alongside scaled partial derivatives at each texel of a padded cubemap. This derivative-augmented representation enables bicubic-Hermite reconstruction from only 4 texture samples (a 2x2 footprint) while providing continuous gradients from the same samples. The key insight is that Hermite interpolation reconstructs smooth derivatives as a byproduct of value reconstruction.
AI.result: Spherical Hermite Maps improve PSNR by 8-41 dB over bilinear interpolation and match 16-tap bicubic quality at one-quarter the cost. Analytic normals reduce mean angular error by 9-13% on complex surfaces while yielding stable specular highlights.
AI.conclusion: The technique demonstrates versatility through three applications: spherical harmonic glyph visualization, radial depth-map impostors for mesh level-of-detail, and procedural planet/asteroid rendering with spherical heightfields.

title: FORMICA: Decision-Focused Learning for Communication-Free Multi-Robot Task Allocation
summary: Most multi-robot task allocation methods rely on communication to resolve conflicts and reach consistent assignments. In environments with limited bandwidth, degraded infrastructure, or adversarial interference, existing approaches degrade sharply. We introduce a learning-based framework that achieves high-quality task allocation without any robot-to-robot communication. The key idea is that robots coordinate implicitly by predicting teammates' bids: if each robot can anticipate competition for a task, it can adjust its choices accordingly. Our method predicts bid distributions to correct systematic errors in analytical mean-field approximations. While analytical predictions assume idealized conditions (uniform distributions, known bid functions), our learned approach adapts to task clustering and spatial heterogeneity. Inspired by Smart Predict-then-Optimize (SPO), we train predictors end-to-end to minimize Task Allocation Regret rather than prediction error. To scale to large swarms, we develop a mean-field approximation where each robot predicts the distribution of competing bids rather than individual bids, reducing complexity from $O(NT)$ to $O(T)$. We call our approach FORMICA: Field-Oriented Regret-Minimizing Implicit Coordination Algorithm. Experiments show FORMICA substantially outperforms a natural analytical baseline. In scenarios with 16 robots and 64 tasks, our approach improves system reward by 17% and approaches the optimal MILP solution. When deployed on larger scenarios (256 robots, 4096 tasks), the same model improves performance by 7%, demonstrating strong generalization. Training requires only 21 seconds on a laptop, enabling rapid adaptation to new environments.

AI.tldr: A learning-based framework called FORMICA for multi-robot task allocation that achieves high-quality assignments without any robot-to-robot communication by predicting teammates' bid distributions and minimizing regret.
AI.motivation: Most multi-robot task allocation methods rely on communication to resolve conflicts, but these approaches degrade sharply in environments with limited bandwidth, degraded infrastructure, or adversarial interference. The paper aims to create a method that achieves high-quality task allocation without communication.
AI.method: Robots coordinate implicitly by predicting bid distributions rather than individual bids. The method uses a mean-field approximation where each robot predicts the distribution of competing bids (reducing complexity from O(NT) to O(T)). Trained end-to-end to minimize Task Allocation Regret rather than prediction error, inspired by Smart Predict-then-Optimize (SPO).
AI.result: In 16-robot/64-task scenarios, improves system reward by 17% and approaches optimal MILP solutions. In larger scenarios (256 robots, 4096 tasks), improves performance by 7%. Training requires only 21 seconds on a laptop.
AI.conclusion: FORMICA achieves high-quality task allocation without communication, scales well to large swarms, generalizes to new environments, and substantially outperforms analytical baselines while being computationally efficient.

title: RotorSuite: A MATLAB/Simulink Toolbox for Tilt Multi-Rotor UAV Modeling
summary: In recent years, aerial platforms have evolved from passive flying sensors into versatile, contact-aware robotic systems, leading to rapid advances in platform design. Standard coplanar and collinear quadrotors have been complemented by modern tilted and tilting multi-rotor platforms with enhanced maneuverability. To properly analyze, control, and validate the performance of these emerging platforms, an accurate modeling step is required; however, this can be time-consuming, user-dependent and error-prone. To address this issue, we propose a MATLAB/Simulink toolbox for modeling and simulating the dynamics of a broad class of multi-rotor platforms through both an analytical and physics-based approaches. The toolbox, named RotorSuite, is provided with comprehensive documentation and example use cases, representing a valuable tool for didactic, research, and industrial development purposes.

AI.tldr: RotorSuite is a MATLAB/Simulink toolbox for modeling and simulating multi-rotor platform dynamics using both analytical and physics-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Aerial platforms have evolved into versatile, contact-aware robotic systems, and modern tilted and tilting multi-rotor platforms require accurate modeling for control and performance validation. However, manual modeling is time-consuming, user-dependent, and error-prone.
AI.method: The authors propose RotorSuite, a MATLAB/Simulink toolbox that models dynamics of a broad class of multi-rotor platforms through both analytical and physics-based approaches, with comprehensive documentation and example use cases.
AI.result: RotorSuite provides a tool that can be used for didactic, research, and industrial development purposes.
AI.conclusion: RotorSuite is a valuable tool for accurately and efficiently modeling multi-rotor dynamics, addressing the challenges associated with manual modeling processes.

title: Prompt Optimization Via Diffusion Language Models
summary: We propose a diffusion-based framework for prompt optimization that leverages Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) to iteratively refine system prompts through masked denoising. By conditioning on interaction traces, including user queries, model responses, and optional feedback, our method enables flexible, span-level prompt updates without requiring gradient access or modifying the downstream language model. Across diverse benchmarks (e.g., $τ$-bench, SST-2, SST-5), DLM-optimized prompts consistently improve the performance of a frozen target LLM (e.g., GPT-4o-mini). We further show that moderate diffusion step counts provide the best balance between refinement quality and stability. These results highlight diffusion-based prompt optimization as a general, model-agnostic, and scalable approach for enhancing LLM performance through iterative prompt refinement.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework for prompt optimization using Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) to iteratively refine system prompts through masked denoising, improving frozen LLM performance without gradient access or model modification.
AI.motivation: The authors seek a model-agnostic, scalable approach to enhance LLM performance through iterative prompt refinement without requiring gradient access or modifying the downstream language model.
AI.method: The method leverages Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) to iteratively refine system prompts via masked denoising, conditioning on interaction traces including user queries, model responses, and optional feedback to enable flexible span-level prompt updates.
AI.result: Across diverse benchmarks (τ-bench, SST-2, SST-5), DLM-optimized prompts consistently improve the performance of a frozen target LLM (e.g., GPT-4o-mini). Moderate diffusion step counts provide the optimal balance between refinement quality and stability.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based prompt optimization is a general, model-agnostic, and scalable approach for enhancing LLM performance through iterative prompt refinement.

title: Mitigating Shortcut Learning via Feature Disentanglement in Medical Imaging: A Benchmark Study
summary: Although deep learning models in medical imaging often achieve excellent classification performance, they can rely on shortcut learning, exploiting spurious correlations or confounding factors that are not causally related to the target task. This poses risks in clinical settings, where models must generalize across institutions, populations, and acquisition conditions. Feature disentanglement is a promising approach to mitigate shortcut learning by separating task-relevant information from confounder-related features in latent representations. In this study, we systematically evaluated feature disentanglement methods for mitigating shortcuts in medical imaging, including adversarial learning and latent space splitting based on dependence minimization. We assessed classification performance and disentanglement quality using latent space analyses across one artificial and two medical datasets with natural and synthetic confounders. We also examined robustness under varying levels of confounding and compared computational efficiency across methods. We found that shortcut mitigation methods improved classification performance under strong spurious correlations during training. Latent space analyses revealed differences in representation quality not captured by classification metrics, highlighting the strengths and limitations of each method. Model reliance on shortcuts depended on the degree of confounding in the training data. The best-performing models combine data-centric rebalancing with model-centric disentanglement, achieving stronger and more robust shortcut mitigation than rebalancing alone while maintaining similar computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Feature disentanglement methods effectively mitigate shortcut learning in medical imaging by separating task-relevant features from confounders, with the best performance achieved by combining disentanglement with data rebalancing.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models in medical imaging often suffer from shortcut learning, exploiting spurious correlations that are not causally related to the target task. This poses risks in clinical settings where models must generalize across institutions, populations, and acquisition conditions. Feature disentanglement is a promising approach to address this issue.
AI.method: The study systematically evaluated feature disentanglement methods for mitigating shortcuts in medical imaging, including adversarial learning and latent space splitting based on dependence minimization. The methods were assessed across one artificial and two medical datasets with natural and synthetic confounders using latent space analyses for both classification performance and disentanglement quality. Robustness under varying confounding levels and computational efficiency were also examined.
AI.result: Shortcut mitigation methods improved classification performance under strong spurious correlations during training. Latent space analyses revealed differences in representation quality not captured by classification metrics. Model reliance on shortcuts depended on the degree of confounding in training data.
AI.conclusion: The best-performing models combine data-centric rebalancing with model-centric disentanglement, achieving stronger and more robust shortcut mitigation than rebalancing alone while maintaining similar computational efficiency.

title: Decoding ML Decision: An Agentic Reasoning Framework for Large-Scale Ranking System
summary: Modern large-scale ranking systems operate within a sophisticated landscape of competing objectives, operational constraints, and evolving product requirements. Progress in this domain is increasingly bottlenecked by the engineering context constraint: the arduous process of translating ambiguous product intent into reasonable, executable, verifiable hypotheses, rather than by modeling techniques alone. We present GEARS (Generative Engine for Agentic Ranking Systems), a framework that reframes ranking optimization as an autonomous discovery process within a programmable experimentation environment. Rather than treating optimization as static model selection, GEARS leverages Specialized Agent Skills to encapsulate ranking expert knowledge into reusable reasoning capabilities, enabling operators to steer systems via high-level intent vibe personalization. Furthermore, to ensure production reliability, the framework incorporates validation hooks to enforce statistical robustness and filter out brittle policies that overfit short-term signals. Experimental validation across diverse product surfaces demonstrates that GEARS consistently identifies superior, near-Pareto-efficient policies by synergizing algorithmic signals with deep ranking context while maintaining rigorous deployment stability.

AI.tldr: GEARS (Generative Engine for Agentic Ranking Systems) transforms ranking optimization into an autonomous discovery process by encapsulating expert knowledge into specialized agent skills, enabling high-level intent steering while ensuring statistical robustness through validation hooks.
AI.motivation: Modern large-scale ranking systems face bottlenecks in translating ambiguous product intent into executable hypotheses due to engineering constraints, not modeling limitations.
AI.method: GEARS reframes ranking optimization as autonomous discovery within a programmable experimentation environment, using specialized agent skills to encapsulate ranking expertise, high-level intent steering capabilities, and validation hooks for statistical robustness and brittle policy filtering.
AI.result: Experimental validation across diverse product surfaces demonstrates GEARS consistently identifies superior, near-Pareto-efficient policies that synergize algorithmic signals with deep ranking context while maintaining rigorous deployment stability.
AI.conclusion: GEARS effectively addresses the engineering context constraint in ranking systems by enabling autonomous discovery of optimal policies through reusable reasoning capabilities and statistical validation mechanisms.

title: Support Vector Data Description for Radar Target Detection
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, to appear in Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP), 2026 IEEE International Conference on, Barcelona, Spain, May 2026
summary: Classical radar detection techniques rely on adaptive detectors that estimate the noise covariance matrix from target-free secondary data. While effective in Gaussian environments, these methods degrade in the presence of clutter, which is better modeled by heavy-tailed distributions such as the Complex Elliptically Symmetric (CES) and Compound-Gaussian (CGD) families. Robust covariance estimators like M-estimators or Tyler's estimator address this issue, but still struggle when thermal noise combines with clutter. To overcome these challenges, we investigate the use of Support Vector Data Description (SVDD) and its deep extension, Deep SVDD, for target detection. These one-class learning methods avoid direct noise covariance estimation and are adapted here as CFAR detectors. We propose two novel SVDD-based detection algorithms and demonstrate their effectiveness on simulated radar data.

AI.tldr: Classical adaptive radar detectors fail in clutter-heavy environments. The authors propose using SVDD and Deep SVDD as one-class learning methods for CFAR detection without covariance estimation, demonstrating effectiveness on simulated radar data.
AI.motivation: Classical adaptive radar detection relies on estimating noise covariance matrix from target-free secondary data, which works well in Gaussian environments but degrades in clutter (modeled by heavy-tailed distributions like CES and CGD). Existing robust covariance estimators (M-estimators, Tyler's estimator) struggle when thermal noise combines with clutter.
AI.method: Investigate Support Vector Data Description (SVDD) and Deep SVDD as one-class learning methods adapted as CFAR detectors, avoiding direct noise covariance estimation. Two novel SVDD-based detection algorithms are proposed and tested on simulated radar data.
AI.result: The effectiveness of the proposed SVDD-based detection algorithms is demonstrated on simulated radar data, showing promise for robust detection in challenging clutter environments.
AI.conclusion: SVDD and Deep SVDD provide a viable alternative to covariance-based adaptive detectors for radar target detection in clutter-heavy environments, successfully avoiding the challenges associated with noise covariance estimation.

title: Adaptive Collaboration of Arena-Based Argumentative LLMs for Explainable and Contestable Legal Reasoning
summary: Legal reasoning requires not only high accuracy but also the ability to justify decisions through verifiable and contestable arguments. However, existing Large Language Model (LLM) approaches, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), often produce unstructured explanations that lack a formal mechanism for verification or user intervention. To address this limitation, we propose Adaptive Collaboration of Argumentative LLMs (ACAL), a neuro-symbolic framework that integrates adaptive multi-agent collaboration with an Arena-based Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework (A-QBAF). ACAL dynamically deploys expert agent teams to construct arguments, employs a clash resolution mechanism to adjudicate conflicting claims, and utilizes uncertainty-aware escalation for borderline cases. Crucially, our framework supports a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) contestability workflow, enabling users to directly audit and modify the underlying reasoning graph to influence the final judgment. Empirical evaluations on the LegalBench benchmark demonstrate that ACAL outperforms strong baselines across Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Gemini-2.5-Flash architectures, effectively balancing efficient predictive performance with structured transparency and contestability. Our implementation is available at: https://github.com/loc110504/ACAL.

title: Optimizing ID Consistency in Multimodal Large Models: Facial Restoration via Alignment, Entanglement, and Disentanglement
summary: Multimodal editing large models have demonstrated powerful editing capabilities across diverse tasks. However, a persistent and long-standing limitation is the decline in facial identity (ID) consistency during realistic portrait editing. Due to the human eye's high sensitivity to facial features, such inconsistency significantly hinders the practical deployment of these models. Current facial ID preservation methods struggle to achieve consistent restoration of both facial identity and edited element IP due to Cross-source Distribution Bias and Cross-source Feature Contamination. To address these issues, we propose EditedID, an Alignment-Disentanglement-Entanglement framework for robust identity-specific facial restoration. By systematically analyzing diffusion trajectories, sampler behaviors, and attention properties, we introduce three key components: 1) Adaptive mixing strategy that aligns cross-source latent representations throughout the diffusion process. 2) Hybrid solver that disentangles source-specific identity attributes and details. 3) Attentional gating mechanism that selectively entangles visual elements. Extensive experiments show that EditedID achieves state-of-the-art performance in preserving original facial ID and edited element IP consistency. As a training-free and plug-and-play solution, it establishes a new benchmark for practical and reliable single/multi-person facial identity restoration in open-world settings, paving the way for the deployment of multimodal editing large models in real-person editing scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/NDYBSNDY/EditedID.

AI.tldr: EditedID is a training-free, plug-and-play framework for preserving facial identity consistency during realistic portrait editing, addressing cross-source distribution bias and feature contamination through alignment-disentanglement-entanglement.
AI.motivation: Multimodal editing large models face a critical limitation in facial identity (ID) consistency during realistic portrait editing. Since the human eye is highly sensitive to facial features, this inconsistency hinders practical deployment. Current facial ID preservation methods struggle due to two main challenges: Cross-source Distribution Bias and Cross-source Feature Contamination, which prevent consistent restoration of both facial identity and edited element intellectual property (IP).
AI.method: EditedID employs an Alignment-Disentanglement-Entanglement framework with three key components: (1) Adaptive mixing strategy that aligns cross-source latent representations throughout the diffusion process; (2) Hybrid solver that disentangles source-specific identity attributes and details; (3) Attentional gating mechanism that selectively entangles visual elements.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that EditedID achieves state-of-the-art performance in preserving original facial identity and edited element IP consistency. As a training-free and plug-and-play solution, it establishes a new benchmark for practical and reliable single/multi-person facial identity restoration in open-world settings.
AI.conclusion: EditedID provides a practical solution for deploying multimodal editing large models in real-person editing scenarios. The method's training-free nature makes it easily applicable, and the code is publicly available at https://github.com/NDYBSNDY/EditedID.

title: Soft Surfaced Vision-Based Tactile Sensing for Bipedal Robot Applications
comment: 8 pages, 11 figures, RoboSoft 2026. For the supplementary video, please visit: https://youtu.be/ceJiy9q_2Aw Section IV-D updated
summary: Legged locomotion benefits from embodied sensing, where perception emerges from the physical interaction between body and environment. We present a soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor that endows a bipedal robot with a skin-like deformable layer that captures contact deformations optically, turning foot-ground interactions into rich haptic signals. From a contact image stream, our method estimates contact pose (position and orientation), visualizes shear, computes center of pressure (CoP), classifies terrain, and detects geometric features of the contact patch. We validate these capabilities on a tilting platform and in visually obscured conditions, showing that foot-borne tactile feedback improves balance control and terrain awareness beyond proprioception alone. These findings suggest that integrating tactile perception into legged robot feet improves stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness, offering a promising direction toward more compliant and intelligent locomotion systems. For the supplementary video, please visit: https://youtu.be/ceJiy9q_2Aw

AI.tldr: A soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor for bipedal robots that captures contact deformations optically, enabling contact pose estimation, shear visualization, CoP computation, terrain classification, and geometric feature detection to improve balance control and terrain awareness.
AI.motivation: Legged locomotion benefits from embodied sensing where perception emerges from physical interaction between body and environment. Current systems rely primarily on proprioception, but tactile feedback could significantly improve stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness.
AI.method: A soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor with a skin-like deformable layer that captures contact deformations optically. The method processes contact image streams to estimate contact pose (position and orientation), visualize shear, compute center of pressure (CoP), classify terrain, and detect geometric features of the contact patch. Validation was performed on a tilting platform and in visually obscured conditions.
AI.result: The foot-borne tactile feedback significantly improved balance control and terrain awareness beyond proprioception alone. The system successfully demonstrated contact pose estimation, shear visualization, CoP computation, terrain classification, and geometric feature detection capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Integrating tactile perception into legged robot feet improves stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness, offering a promising direction toward more compliant and intelligent locomotion systems.

title: Depth-Enhanced YOLO-SAM2 Detection for Reliable Ballast Insufficiency Identification
comment: Submitted to the IEEE International Symposium on Robotic and Sensors Environments (ROSE) 2026
summary:
This paper presents a depth-enhanced YOLO-SAM2 framework for detecting ballast insufficiency in railway tracks using RGB-D data. Although YOLOv8 provides reliable localization, the RGB-only model shows limited safety performance, achieving high precision (0.99) but low recall (0.49) due to insufficient ballast, as it tends to over-predict the sufficient class. To improve reliability, we incorporate depth-based geometric analysis enabled by a sleeper-aligned depth-correction pipeline that compensates for RealSense spatial distortion using polynomial modeling, RANSAC, and temporal smoothing. SAM2 segmentation further refines region-of-interest masks, enabling accurate extraction of sleeper and ballast profiles for geometric classification.
  Experiments on field-collected top-down RGB-D data show that depth-enhanced configurations substantially improve the detection of insufficient ballast. Depending on bounding-box sampling (AABB or RBB) and geometric criteria, recall increases from 0.49 to as high as 0.80, and F1-score improves from 0.66 to over 0.80. These results demonstrate that integrating depth correction with YOLO-SAM2 yields a more robust and reliable approach for automated railway ballast inspection, particularly in visually ambiguous or safety-critical scenarios.

AI.tldr: A depth-enhanced YOLO-SAM2 framework using RGB-D data improves ballast insufficiency detection in railway tracks, increasing recall from 0.49 to 0.80 and F1-score from 0.66 to over 0.80 through depth correction and geometric analysis.
AI.motivation: RGB-only YOLOv8 shows limited safety performance (high precision 0.99 but low recall 0.49) due to over-prediction of sufficient ballast when ballast is insufficient.
AI.method: Incorporates depth-based geometric analysis with a sleeper-aligned depth-correction pipeline that compensates RealSense spatial distortion using polynomial modeling, RANSAC, and temporal smoothing. SAM2 segmentation refines region-of-interest masks for accurate extraction of sleeper and ballast profiles.
AI.result: Recall increases from 0.49 to as high as 0.80, and F1-score improves from 0.66 to over 0.80 depending on bounding-box sampling (AABB or RBB) and geometric criteria.
AI.conclusion: Integrating depth correction with YOLO-SAM2 yields a more robust and reliable approach for automated railway ballast inspection, particularly in visually ambiguous or safety-critical scenarios.

title: Asymptotic Semantic Collapse in Hierarchical Optimization
comment: 23 pages, 2 figures. Includes a dataset-free benchmark with full metric reporting
summary: Multi-agent language systems can exhibit a failure mode where a shared dominant context progressively absorbs individual semantics, yielding near-uniform behavior across agents. We study this effect under the name Asymptotic Semantic Collapse in Hierarchical Optimization. In a closed linguistic setting with a Dominant Anchor Node whose semantic state has effectively infinite inertia, we show that repeated interactions with Peripheral Agent Nodes drive an asymptotic alignment that minimizes a global loss. We model semantic states as points on a Riemannian manifold and analyze the induced projection dynamics. Two consequences follow. First, the limiting semantic configuration is insensitive to the optimization history: both smooth gradient-style updates and stochastic noisy updates converge to the same topological endpoint, establishing path independence at convergence. Second, the degree of context dependence controls information content: moving from atomic (independent) representations to fully entangled (context-bound) representations forces the node entropy, interpreted as available degrees of freedom, to vanish in the limit. The theory connects information-theoretic quantities with differential-geometric structure and suggests an interpretation as an immutable consensus rule that constrains agents to a shared semantic grammar. A lightweight dataset-free benchmark on an RWKV-7 13B GGUF checkpoint complements the analysis, reporting zero hash collisions, mean compliance of 0.50 under greedy decoding and 0.531 under stochastic decoding, and final Jaccard-to-anchor similarity values of 0.295 and 0.224, respectively.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent language systems exhibit a failure mode called "Asymptotic Semantic Collapse in Hierarchical Optimization" where a shared dominant context progressively absorbs individual semantics, leading to near-uniform behavior across agents.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates a specific failure mode in multi-agent language systems where a shared dominant context progressively absorbs individual semantics, reducing behavioral diversity.
AI.method: Models semantic states as points on a Riemannian manifold within a closed linguistic setting containing a Dominant Anchor Node (with infinite inertia) and Peripheral Agent Nodes. Analyzes induced projection dynamics under repeated interactions, comparing smooth gradient-style and stochastic noisy updates.
AI.result: Repeated interactions drive asymptotic alignment minimizing global loss. The limiting semantic configuration is path-independent (insensitive to optimization history). Moving from atomic to entangled representations causes node entropy to vanish. Benchmark results: zero hash collisions, mean compliance 0.50 (greedy) and 0.531 (stochastic), Jaccard-to-anchor similarity 0.295 (greedy) and 0.224 (stochastic).
AI.conclusion: The theory connects information-theoretic quantities with differential-geometric structure, suggesting an interpretation as an immutable consensus rule that constrains agents to a shared semantic grammar.

title: A Computer Vision Framework for Multi-Class Detection and Tracking in Soccer Broadcast Footage
comment: Presented at the Robyn Rafferty Mathias Reseaerch Conference. Additional Information available at: https://DGT-International.com
summary: Clubs with access to expensive multi-camera setups or GPS tracking systems gain a competitive advantage through detailed data, whereas lower-budget teams are often unable to collect similar information. This paper examines whether such data can instead be extracted directly from standard broadcast footage using a single-camera computer vision pipeline. This project develops an end-to-end system that combines a YOLO object detector with the ByteTrack tracking algorithm to identify and track players, referees, goalkeepers, and the ball throughout a match. Experimental results show that the pipeline achieves high performance in detecting and tracking players and officials, with strong precision, recall, and mAP50 scores, while ball detection remains the primary challenge. Despite this limitation, our findings demonstrate that AI can extract meaningful player-level spatial information from a single broadcast camera. By reducing reliance on specialized hardware, the proposed approach enables colleges, academies, and amateur clubs to adopt scalable, data-driven analysis methods previously accessible only to professional teams, highlighting the potential for affordable computer vision-based soccer analytics.

AI.tldr: A single-camera computer vision pipeline using YOLO object detector and ByteTrack tracking algorithm extracts player-level spatial information from broadcast footage, making data-driven soccer analytics accessible to lower-budget teams.
AI.motivation: Clubs with expensive multi-camera setups or GPS tracking systems gain competitive advantages through detailed data, while lower-budget teams are unable to collect similar information. The study examines whether detailed data can be extracted from standard broadcast footage using a single-camera approach.
AI.method: An end-to-end system combining a YOLO object detector with the ByteTrack tracking algorithm to identify and track players, referees, goalkeepers, and the ball throughout a match from a single broadcast camera.
AI.result: The pipeline achieves high performance in detecting and tracking players and officials with strong precision, recall, and mAP50 scores, while ball detection remains the primary challenge. AI successfully extracts meaningful player-level spatial information from a single broadcast camera.
AI.conclusion: By reducing reliance on specialized hardware, the approach enables colleges, academies, and amateur clubs to adopt scalable, data-driven analysis methods previously accessible only to professional teams, highlighting the potential for affordable computer vision-based soccer analytics.

title: Spilled Energy in Large Language Models
summary: We reinterpret the final Large Language Model (LLM) softmax classifier as an Energy-Based Model (EBM), decomposing the sequence-to-sequence probability chain into multiple interacting EBMs at inference. This principled approach allows us to track "energy spills" during decoding, which we empirically show correlate with factual errors, biases, and failures. Similar to Orgad et al. (2025), our method localizes the exact answer token and subsequently tests for hallucinations. Crucially, however, we achieve this without requiring trained probe classifiers or activation ablations. Instead, we introduce two completely training-free metrics derived directly from output logits: spilled energy, which captures the discrepancy between energy values across consecutive generation steps that should theoretically match, and marginalized energy, which is measurable at a single step. Evaluated on nine benchmarks across state-of-the-art LLMs (including LLaMA, Mistral, and Gemma) and on synthetic algebraic operations (Qwen3), our approach demonstrates robust, competitive hallucination detection and cross-task generalization. Notably, these results hold for both pretrained and instruction-tuned variants without introducing any training overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets LLM softmax classifiers as Energy-Based Models (EBMs) and introduces two training-free metrics (spilled energy and marginalized energy) to detect hallucinations by tracking "energy spills" during decoding, achieving robust detection across multiple SOTA LLMs without requiring probe classifiers or activation ablations.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination detection methods require trained probe classifiers or activation ablations, which are computationally expensive and task-specific. The authors seek a principled, training-free approach to identify factual errors, biases, and failures in LLM outputs.
AI.method:
1. Reinterprets the final LLM softmax classifier as an Energy-Based Model (EBM)
2. Decomposes the sequence-to-sequence probability chain into multiple interacting EBMs at inference
3. Introduces "spilled energy" metric: captures discrepancy between energy values across consecutive generation steps that should theoretically match
4. Introduces "marginalized energy" metric: measurable at a single step
5. Localizes exact answer token and tests for hallucinations without trained probes
AI.result: Evaluated on nine benchmarks across SOTA LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma) and synthetic algebraic operations (Qwen3), the approach demonstrates robust, competitive hallucination detection with cross-task generalization. Results hold for both pretrained and instruction-tuned variants.
AI.conclusion: Energy spill correlation with factual errors provides a practical, training-free approach for hallucination detection that generalizes across tasks and model variants, eliminating the need for probe classifiers or activation ablations.

title: Learning to Remember: End-to-End Training of Memory Agents for Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Long-context LLMs and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems process information passively, deferring state tracking, contradiction resolution, and evidence aggregation to query time, which becomes brittle under ultra long streams with frequent updates. We propose the Unified Memory Agent (UMA), an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that unifies memory operations and question answering within a single policy. UMA maintains a dual memory representation: a compact core summary for global context and a structured Memory Bank that supports explicit CRUD (create, update, delete, reorganize) over key value entries, enabling proactive consolidation during streaming. To evaluate long-horizon memory behavior, we introduce Ledger-QA, a diagnostic benchmark for continuous state tracking where answers are latent values derived from accumulated updates rather than lo cal span retrieval. Across 13 datasets spanning Ledger-QA, Test-Time Learning, and Accurate Retrieval, UMA substantially outperforms long-context and RAG baselines on dynamic reasoning and learning tasks while remaining competitive on standard retrieval benchmarks, underscoring the importance of learned, end-to-end memory management.

AI.tldr: UMA is an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that unifies memory operations and question answering with dual memory representation (compact summary + structured Memory Bank) to handle ultra-long streams with frequent updates, outperforming long-context and RAG baselines on dynamic reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional long-context LLMs and RAG systems process information passively, deferring state tracking, contradiction resolution, and evidence aggregation to query time, which becomes brittle under ultra-long streams with frequent updates.
AI.method: UMA uses an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework with dual memory representation: a compact core summary for global context and a structured Memory Bank supporting explicit CRUD operations over key-value entries, enabling proactive consolidation. The authors also introduce Ledger-QA, a diagnostic benchmark for continuous state tracking with latent value answers.
AI.result: UMA substantially outperforms long-context and RAG baselines on dynamic reasoning and learning tasks across 13 datasets spanning Ledger-QA, Test-Time Learning, and Accurate Retrieval, while remaining competitive on standard retrieval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Learned, end-to-end memory management is critical for effective long-horizon memory behavior in ultra-long streams with frequent updates.

title: A potentialization algorithm for games with applications to multi-agent learning in repeated games
summary: We investigate an algorithm that assigns to any game in normal form an approximating game that admits an ordinal potential function. Due to the properties of potential games, the algorithm equips every game with a surrogate reward structure that allows efficient multi-agent learning. Numerical simulations using the replicator dynamics show that 'potentialization' guarantees convergence to stable agent behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an algorithm to convert any normal-form game into an approximating game with an ordinal potential function, enabling efficient multi-agent learning and ensuring convergence to stable behavior via replicator dynamics.
AI.motivation: To equip every game with a surrogate reward structure that allows efficient multi-agent learning, addressing the challenge that not all games naturally possess potential functions which are desirable for learning algorithms.
AI.method: An algorithm that assigns to any normal-form game an approximating game that admits an ordinal potential function (a process called "potentialization").
AI.result: Numerical simulations using replicator dynamics demonstrate that potentialization guarantees convergence to stable agent behavior.
AI.conclusion: Potentialization successfully transforms arbitrary games into games with potential functions, enabling efficient learning and ensuring convergence to equilibrium.

title: PhysConvex: Physics-Informed 3D Dynamic Convex Radiance Fields for Reconstruction and Simulation
summary: Reconstructing and simulating dynamic 3D scenes with both visual realism and physical consistency remains a fundamental challenge. Existing neural representations, such as NeRFs and 3DGS, excel in appearance reconstruction but struggle to capture complex material deformation and dynamics. We propose PhysConvex, a Physics-informed 3D Dynamic Convex Radiance Field that unifies visual rendering and physical simulation. PhysConvex represents deformable radiance fields using physically grounded convex primitives governed by continuum mechanics. We introduce a boundary-driven dynamic convex representation that models deformation through vertex and surface dynamics, capturing spatially adaptive, non-uniform deformation, and evolving boundaries. To efficiently simulate complex geometries and heterogeneous materials, we further develop a reduced-order convex simulation that advects dynamic convex fields using neural skinning eigenmodes as shape- and material-aware deformation bases with time-varying reduced DOFs under Newtonian dynamics. Convex dynamics also offers compact, gap-free volumetric coverage, enhancing both geometric efficiency and simulation fidelity. Experiments demonstrate that PhysConvex achieves high-fidelity reconstruction of geometry, appearance, and physical properties from videos, outperforming existing methods.

AI.tldr: PhysConvex is a Physics-informed 3D Dynamic Convex Radiance Field that unifies visual rendering and physical simulation using convex primitives governed by continuum mechanics. It introduces a boundary-driven dynamic convex representation and a reduced-order convex simulation with neural skinning eigenmodes to efficiently capture complex deformations and heterogeneous materials, achieving high-fidelity reconstruction of geometry, appearance, and physical properties from videos.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing and simulating dynamic 3D scenes with both visual realism and physical consistency is a fundamental challenge. Existing neural representations (NeRFs, 3DGS) excel at appearance reconstruction but struggle to capture complex material deformation and dynamics, limiting their ability to produce physically consistent simulations.
AI.method: PhysConvex employs physically grounded convex primitives governed by continuum mechanics. It introduces a boundary-driven dynamic convex representation that models deformation through vertex and surface dynamics, capturing spatially adaptive, non-uniform deformation with evolving boundaries. For efficient simulation of complex geometries and heterogeneous materials, it develops a reduced-order convex simulation that advects dynamic convex fields using neural skinning eigenmodes as shape- and material-aware deformation bases with time-varying reduced degrees of freedom under Newtonian dynamics.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that PhysConvex achieves high-fidelity reconstruction of geometry, appearance, and physical properties from videos, outperforming existing methods in reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes with complex material deformations.
AI.conclusion: PhysConvlex successfully unifies visual rendering and physical simulation through its physics-informed convex radiance field approach, demonstrating superior performance in reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes while maintaining physical consistency. The method effectively captures complex material deformations and evolving boundaries, offering a promising direction for bridging the gap between visual realism and physical simulation in dynamic scene reconstruction.

title: Distributed and Consistent Multi-Robot Visual-Inertial-Ranging Odometry on Lie Groups
summary: Reliable localization is a fundamental requirement for multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied environments. Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) provides lightweight and accurate motion estimation but suffers from cumulative drift in the absence of global references. Ultra-wideband (UWB) ranging offers complementary global observations, yet most existing UWB-aided VIO methods are designed for single-robot scenarios and rely on pre-calibrated anchors, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper proposes a distributed collaborative visual-inertial-ranging odometry (DC-VIRO) framework that tightly fuses VIO and UWB measurements across multiple robots. Anchor positions are explicitly included in the system state to address calibration uncertainty, while shared anchor observations are exploited through inter-robot communication to provide additional geometric constraints. By leveraging a right-invariant error formulation on Lie groups, the proposed approach preserves the observability properties of standard VIO, ensuring estimator consistency. Simulation results with multiple robots demonstrate that DC-VIRO significantly improves localization accuracy and robustness, while simultaneously enabling anchor self-calibration in distributed settings.

AI.tldr: A distributed collaborative visual-inertial-ranging odometry (DC-VIRO) framework that tightly fuses VIO and UWB measurements across multiple robots, explicitly including anchor positions in the system state and exploiting shared anchor observations through inter-robot communication to address calibration uncertainty and improve localization accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reliable localization is essential for multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied environments. While visual-inertial odometry (VIO) provides lightweight and accurate motion estimation, it suffers from cumulative drift without global references. Ultra-wideband (UWB) ranging offers complementary global observations, but existing UWB-aided VIO methods are designed for single-robot scenarios and rely on pre-calibrated anchors, limiting their robustness in practice.
AI.method: The proposed DC-VIRO framework tightly fuses VIO and UWB measurements across multiple robots. Anchor positions are explicitly included in the system state to address calibration uncertainty, while shared anchor observations are exploited through inter-robot communication to provide additional geometric constraints. The approach uses a right-invariant error formulation on Lie groups to preserve the observability properties of standard VIO, ensuring estimator consistency.
AI.result: Simulation results with multiple robots demonstrate that DC-VIRO significantly improves localization accuracy and robustness, while simultaneously enabling anchor self-calibration in distributed settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach preserves the observability properties of standard VIO, ensuring estimator consistency, and effectively addresses the limitations of existing UWB-aided VIO methods in multi-robot scenarios.

title: The Million-Label NER: Breaking Scale Barriers with GLiNER bi-encoder
summary: This paper introduces GLiNER-bi-Encoder, a novel architecture for Named Entity Recognition (NER) that harmonizes zero-shot flexibility with industrial-scale efficiency. While the original GLiNER framework offers strong generalization, its joint-encoding approach suffers from quadratic complexity as the number of entity labels increases. Our proposed bi-encoder design decouples the process into a dedicated label encoder and a context encoder, effectively removing the context-window bottleneck. This architecture enables the simultaneous recognition of thousands, and potentially millions, of entity types with minimal overhead. Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art zero-shot performance, achieving 61.5 percent Micro-F1 on the CrossNER benchmark. Crucially, by leveraging pre-computed label embeddings, GLiNER-bi-Encoder achieves up to a 130 times throughput improvement at 1024 labels compared to its uni-encoder predecessors. Furthermore, we introduce GLiNKER, a modular framework that leverages this architecture for high-performance entity linking across massive knowledge bases such as Wikidata.

AI.tldr: GLiNER-bi-Encoder introduces a bi-encoder architecture for Named Entity Recognition (NER) that decouples label and context encoding, achieving 130x throughput improvement while maintaining state-of-the-art zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: The original GLiNER framework, while offering strong zero-shot generalization, suffered from quadratic complexity due to its joint-encoding approach as the number of entity labels increased, creating a bottleneck for industrial-scale applications requiring recognition of thousands to millions of entity types.
AI.method: The paper proposes a bi-encoder design that decouples the NER process into a dedicated label encoder and a context encoder, eliminating the context-window bottleneck. They also introduce GLiNKER, a modular framework for entity linking across massive knowledge bases like Wikidata.
AI.result: State-of-the-art zero-shot performance with 61.5% Micro-F1 on the CrossNER benchmark, and up to a 130-fold throughput improvement at 1024 labels compared to uni-encoder predecessors.
AI.conclusion: The bi-encoder architecture enables efficient simultaneous recognition of thousands and potentially millions of entity types with minimal overhead, making it suitable for industrial-scale NER applications requiring high throughput and zero-shot flexibility.

title: Suppression or Deletion: A Restoration-Based Representation-Level Analysis of Machine Unlearning
summary: As pretrained models are increasingly shared on the web, ensuring that models can forget or delete sensitive, copyrighted, or private information upon request has become crucial. Machine unlearning has been proposed to address this challenge. However, current evaluations for unlearning methods rely on output-based metrics, which cannot verify whether information is completely deleted or merely suppressed at the representation level, where suppression is insufficient for true unlearning. To address this gap, we propose a novel restoration-based analysis framework that uses Sparse Autoencoders to identify class-specific expert features in intermediate layers and applies inference-time steering to quantitatively distinguish between suppression and deletion. Applying our framework to 12 major unlearning methods in image classification tasks, we find that most methods achieve high restoration rates of unlearned information, indicating that they only suppress information at the decision-boundary level, while preserving semantic features in intermediate representations. Notably, even retraining from pretrained checkpoints shows high restoration, revealing that robust semantic features inherited from pretraining are not removed by retraining. These results demonstrate that representation-level retention poses significant risks overlooked by output-based metrics, highlighting the need for new unlearning evaluation criteria. We propose new evaluation guidelines that prioritize representation-level verification, especially for privacy-critical applications in the era of pre-trained models.

AI.tldr: A novel restoration-based analysis framework using Sparse Autoencoders to detect whether machine unlearning methods truly delete sensitive information or merely suppress it at the decision boundary, revealing that most methods fail to remove semantic features.
AI.motivation: Current machine unlearning evaluations rely solely on output-based metrics that cannot verify whether information is completely deleted or merely suppressed at the representation level, where suppression is insufficient for true unlearning.
AI.method: Proposes a restoration-based analysis framework that uses Sparse Autoencoders to identify class-specific expert features in intermediate layers and applies inference-time steering to quantitatively distinguish between suppression and deletion.
AI.result: Applying the framework to 12 major unlearning methods in image classification tasks reveals that most achieve high restoration rates, indicating they only suppress information at the decision-boundary level while preserving semantic features in intermediate representations; even retraining from pretrained checkpoints retains robust semantic features.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level retention poses significant risks overlooked by output-based metrics, demonstrating the urgent need for new unlearning evaluation criteria that prioritize representation-level verification, particularly for privacy-critical applications in the era of pretrained models.

title: Many AI Analysts, One Dataset: Navigating the Agentic Data Science Multiverse
summary: The conclusions of empirical research depend not only on data but on a sequence of analytic decisions that published results seldom make explicit. Past ``many-analyst" studies have demonstrated this: independent teams testing the same hypothesis on the same dataset regularly reach conflicting conclusions. But such studies require months of coordination among dozens of research groups and are therefore rarely conducted. In this work, we show that fully autonomous AI analysts built on large language models (LLMs) can reproduce a similar structured analytic diversity cheaply and at scale. We task these AI analysts with testing a pre-specified hypothesis on a fixed dataset, varying the underlying model and prompt framing across replicate runs. Each AI analyst independently constructs and executes a full analysis pipeline; an AI auditor then screens each run for methodological validity. Across three datasets spanning experimental and observational designs, AI analyst-produced analyses display wide dispersion in effect sizes, $p$-values, and binary decisions on supporting the hypothesis or not, frequently reversing whether a hypothesis is judged supported. This dispersion is structured: recognizable analytic choices in preprocessing, model specification, and inference differ systematically across LLM and persona conditions. Critically, the effects are \emph{steerable}: reassigning the analyst persona or LLM shifts the distribution of outcomes even after excluding methodologically deficient runs.

AI.tldr: Fully autonomous AI analysts based on large language models can replicate the phenomenon of conflicting conclusions across independent analysts testing the same hypothesis, demonstrating structured analytic diversity that is steerable through persona and model selection.
AI.motivation: Empirical research conclusions depend on analytic decisions rarely made explicit. Past "many-analyst" studies show independent teams reach conflicting conclusions, but these require months of coordination among dozens of groups and are therefore rarely conducted. The authors seek to demonstrate this phenomenon cheaply and at scale using AI.
AI.method: Fully autonomous AI analysts (LLMs) are tasked with testing a pre-specified hypothesis on a fixed dataset, varying the underlying model and prompt framing across replicate runs. Each AI independently constructs and executes a full analysis pipeline; an AI auditor screens each run for methodological validity.
AI.result: Across three datasets spanning experimental and observational designs, AI analyst-produced analyses show wide dispersion in effect sizes, p-values, and binary decisions on hypothesis support, frequently reversing whether a hypothesis is judged supported. This dispersion is structured by recognizable analytic choices in preprocessing, model specification, and inference. The effects are steerable: reassigning the analyst persona or LLM shifts the distribution of outcomes even after excluding methodologically deficient runs.
AI.conclusion: AI analysts can replicate the phenomenon of conflicting conclusions across multiple analysts, demonstrating that analytic diversity emerges from specific choices and is steerable through researcher characteristics, highlighting the need for transparency in analytic decisions.

title: Weak-Form Evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Solving Partial Differential Equations
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) form a central component of scientific computing. Among recent advances in deep learning, evolutionary neural networks have been developed to successively capture the temporal dynamics of time-dependent PDEs via parameter evolution. The parameter updates are obtained by solving a linear system derived from the governing equation residuals at each time step. However, strong-form evolutionary approaches can yield ill-conditioned linear systems due to pointwise residual discretization, and their computational cost scales unfavorably with the number of training samples. To address these limitations, we propose a weak-form evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) for the scalable and accurate prediction of PDE solutions. We decouple the linear system size from the number of training samples through the weak formulation, leading to improved scalability compared to strong-form approaches. We also rigorously enforce boundary conditions by constructing the trial space with boundary-constrained KANs to satisfy Dirichlet and periodic conditions, and by incorporating derivative boundary conditions directly into the weak formulation for Neumann conditions. In conclusion, the proposed weak-form evolutionary KAN framework provides a stable and scalable approach for PDEs and contributes to scientific machine learning with potential relevance to future engineering applications.

AI.tldr: Weak-form evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) for solving PDEs, improving scalability and accuracy over strong-form approaches by decoupling linear system size from training samples and rigorously enforcing boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: Evolutionary neural networks capture temporal dynamics of time-dependent PDEs via parameter evolution, but strong-form approaches yield ill-conditioned linear systems and poor computational scaling with training samples.
AI.method: Weak-form evolutionary KAN framework decouples linear system size from number of training samples via weak formulation; enforces boundary conditions through boundary-constrained KAN trial space (Dirichlet/periodic) and direct incorporation of derivative boundary conditions (Neumann) into weak formulation.
AI.result: Provides stable and scalable approach for PDEs with improved conditioning and computational efficiency compared to strong-form methods.
AI.conclusion: Weak-form evolutionary KAN framework contributes to scientific machine learning with potential relevance to future engineering applications.

title: Scaling Inference-Time Computation via Opponent Simulation: Enabling Online Strategic Adaptation in Repeated Negotiation
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful decision-makers across a wide range of single-agent and stationary environments, fewer efforts have been devoted to settings where LLMs must engage in \emph{repeated} and \emph{strategic} interactions with unknown or dynamic opponents. In such settings, recipes built upon \emph{offline} pre-training or fine-tuning, though robust against worst-case adversaries, do not fully exploit the capability of LLMs to adapt \emph{online} based on interaction feedback. Instead, we explore the more natural perspective of scaling inference-time computation as a mechanism for adaptation, embedding the principles of a classical game-theoretical learning dynamic, \emph{smooth Fictitious Play (sFP)}, into LLM inference: (i) for belief formation, we employ an auxiliary opponent model that in-context learns to imitate the time-averaged behavior of the opponent; (ii) for best response, we advance best-of-$N$ (BoN) sampling by simulating against the opponent model. Empirical evaluations on two distinct forms of repeated negotiation games demonstrate that our method enables significant performance improvement over repeated online interaction compared to various baselines, offering a scalable and principled approach to repeated strategic decision-making without any parameter updates.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method that embeds smooth Fictitious Play (sFP) into LLM inference to enable online adaptation in repeated strategic interactions without parameter updates, using an auxiliary opponent model for belief formation and best-of-N sampling against that model.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel in single-agent/stationary settings but are underexplored for repeated strategic interactions with unknown/dynamic opponents; existing offline pre-training/fine-tuning approaches are robust but fail to fully exploit LLMs' online adaptation capabilities.
AI.method: The authors embed smooth Fictitious Play (sFP) into LLM inference through two components: (1) belief formation via an auxiliary opponent model that in-context learns to imitate the opponent's time-averaged behavior; (2) best response via advanced best-of-N (BoN) sampling that simulates actions against the opponent model.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on two repeated negotiation games demonstrate significant performance improvement over repeated online interaction and various baselines, showing the method's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a scalable and principled framework for repeated strategic decision-making without any parameter updates, effectively leveraging inference-time computation for online adaptation.

title: Ani3DHuman: Photorealistic 3D Human Animation with Self-guided Stochastic Sampling
summary: Current 3D human animation methods struggle to achieve photorealism: kinematics-based approaches lack non-rigid dynamics (e.g., clothing dynamics), while methods that leverage video diffusion priors can synthesize non-rigid motion but suffer from quality artifacts and identity loss. To overcome these limitations, we present Ani3DHuman, a framework that marries kinematics-based animation with video diffusion priors. We first introduce a layered motion representation that disentangles rigid motion from residual non-rigid motion. Rigid motion is generated by a kinematic method, which then produces a coarse rendering to guide the video diffusion model in generating video sequences that restore the residual non-rigid motion. However, this restoration task, based on diffusion sampling, is highly challenging, as the initial renderings are out-of-distribution, causing standard deterministic ODE samplers to fail. Therefore, we propose a novel self-guided stochastic sampling method, which effectively addresses the out-of-distribution problem by combining stochastic sampling (for photorealistic quality) with self-guidance (for identity fidelity). These restored videos provide high-quality supervision, enabling the optimization of the residual non-rigid motion field. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \MethodName can generate photorealistic 3D human animation, outperforming existing methods. Code is available in https://github.com/qiisun/ani3dhuman.

AI.tldr: Ani3DHuman is a framework that combines kinematics-based animation with video diffusion priors to generate photorealistic 3D human animation by disentangling rigid and non-rigid motion and employing a novel self-guided stochastic sampling method.
AI.motivation: Current 3D human animation methods struggle with photorealism: kinematics-based approaches lack non-rigid dynamics (e.g., clothing), while video diffusion priors can synthesize non-rigid motion but suffer from quality artifacts and identity loss.
AI.method: The framework introduces a layered motion representation that disentangles rigid motion from residual non-rigid motion. Rigid motion is generated by a kinematic method, which produces a coarse rendering to guide a video diffusion model in restoring the residual non-rigid motion.
