title: Human-Guided Agentic AI for Multimodal Clinical Prediction: Lessons from the AgentDS Healthcare Benchmark
comment: Submitted to the Data Challenge track at the 14th IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI) 2026
summary: Agentic AI systems are increasingly capable of autonomous data science workflows, yet clinical prediction tasks demand domain expertise that purely automated approaches struggle to provide. We investigate how human guidance of agentic AI can improve multimodal clinical prediction, presenting our approach to all three AgentDS Healthcare benchmark challenges: 30-day hospital readmission prediction (Macro-F1 = 0.8986), emergency department cost forecasting (MAE = $465.13), and discharge readiness assessment (Macro-F1 = 0.7939). Across these tasks, human analysts directed the agentic workflow at key decision points, multimodal feature engineering from clinical notes, scanned PDF billing receipts, and time-series vital signs; task-appropriate model selection; and clinically informed validation strategies. Our approach ranked 5th overall in the healthcare domain, with a 3rd-place finish on the discharge readiness task. Ablation studies reveal that human-guided decisions compounded to a cumulative gain of +0.065 F1 over automated baselines, with multimodal feature extraction contributing the largest single improvement (+0.041 F1). We distill three generalizable lessons: (1) domain-informed feature engineering at each pipeline stage yields compounding gains that outperform extensive automated search; (2) multimodal data integration requires task-specific human judgment that no single extraction strategy generalizes across clinical text, PDFs, and time-series; and (3) deliberate ensemble diversity with clinically motivated model configurations outperforms random hyperparameter search. These findings offer practical guidance for teams deploying agentic AI in healthcare settings where interpretability, reproducibility, and clinical validity are essential.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a human-guided agentic AI approach for clinical prediction tasks, achieving competitive performance across three healthcare benchmarks (30-day readmission, ED cost forecasting, discharge readiness) with human intervention delivering significant gains over fully automated methods.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems are capable of autonomous data science workflows, but clinical prediction tasks require domain expertise that purely automated approaches struggle to provide. The authors investigate how human guidance of agentic AI can improve multimodal clinical prediction.
AI.method: The approach involves human analysts directing agentic workflows at key decision points across three benchmark tasks: (1) 30-day hospital readmission prediction, (2) emergency department cost forecasting, and (3) discharge readiness assessment. Key components include multimodal feature engineering from clinical notes, scanned PDF billing receipts, and time-series vital signs; task-appropriate model selection; and clinically informed validation strategies.
AI.result: The approach ranked 5th overall in the healthcare domain (3rd place on discharge readiness task). Results include Macro-F1 = 0.8986 for 30-day readmission, MAE = $465.13 for ED cost forecasting, and Macro-F1 = 0.7939 for discharge readiness. Human-guided decisions provided cumulative gain of +0.065 F1 over automated baselines, with multimodal feature extraction contributing largest improvement (+0.041 F1).
AI.conclusion: Three generalizable lessons: (1) domain-informed feature engineering at each pipeline stage yields compounding gains outperforming extensive automated search; (2) multimodal data integration requires task-specific human judgment as no single extraction strategy generalizes across clinical text, PDFs, and time-series; (3) deliberate ensemble diversity with clinically motivated model configurations outperforms random hyperparameter search. The findings offer practical guidance for deploying agentic AI in healthcare where interpretability, reproducibility, and clinical validity are essential.

title: TRUE: A Trustworthy Unified Explanation Framework for Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, yet their decision-making processes remain difficult to interpret. Existing explanation methods often lack trustworthy structural insight and are limited to single-instance analysis, failing to reveal reasoning stability and systematic failure mechanisms. To address these limitations, we propose the Trustworthy Unified Explanation Framework (TRUE), which integrates executable reasoning verification, feasible-region directed acyclic graph (DAG) modeling, and causal failure mode analysis. At the instance level, we redefine reasoning traces as executable process specifications and introduce blind execution verification to assess operational validity. At the local structural level, we construct feasible-region DAGs via structure-consistent perturbations, enabling explicit characterization of reasoning stability and the executable region in the local input space. At the class level, we introduce a causal failure mode analysis method that identifies recurring structural failure patterns and quantifies their causal influence using Shapley values. Extensive experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework provides multi-level, verifiable explanations, including executable reasoning structures for individual instances, feasible-region representations for neighboring inputs, and interpretable failure modes with quantified importance at the class level. These results establish a unified and principled paradigm for improving the interpretability and reliability of LLM reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Trustworthy Unified Explanation Framework (TRUE), which provides multi-level verifiable explanations for LLM reasoning by integrating executable verification, feasible-region DAG modeling, and causal failure mode analysis.
AI.motivation: LLMs demonstrate strong reasoning capabilities but their decision-making processes remain difficult to interpret. Existing explanation methods lack trustworthy structural insight and are limited to single-instance analysis, failing to reveal reasoning stability and systematic failure mechanisms.
AI.method: The framework integrates three complementary approaches: 1) Instance level: redefines reasoning traces as executable process specifications with blind execution verification; 2) Local structural level: constructs feasible-region directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) via structure-consistent perturbations; 3) Class level: introduces causal failure mode analysis using Shapley values to quantify structural failure patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TRUE provides executable reasoning structures for individual instances, feasible-region representations for neighboring inputs, and interpretable failure modes with quantified importance at the class level.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework establishes a unified and principled paradigm for improving the interpretability and reliability of LLM reasoning systems by providing multi-level, verifiable explanations that bridge the gap between model performance and human understanding.

title: CACTO-BIC: Scalable Actor-Critic Learning via Biased Sampling and GPU-Accelerated Trajectory Optimization
summary: Trajectory Optimization (TO) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) offer complementary strengths for solving optimal control problems. TO efficiently computes locally optimal solutions but can struggle with non-convexity, while RL is more robust to non-convexity at the cost of significantly higher computational demands. CACTO (Continuous Actor-Critic with Trajectory Optimization) was introduced to combine these advantages by learning a warm-start policy that guides the TO solver towards low-cost trajectories. However, scalability remains a key limitation, as increasing system complexity significantly raises the computational cost of TO. This work introduces CACTO-BIC to address these challenges. CACTO-BIC improves data efficiency by biasing initial-state sampling leveraging a property of the value function associated with locally optimal policies; moreover, it reduces computation time by exploiting GPU acceleration. Empirical evaluations show improved sample efficiency and faster computation compared to CACTO. Comparisons with PPO demonstrate that our approach can achieve similar solutions in less time. Finally, experiments on the AlienGO quadruped robot demonstrate that CACTO-BIC can scale to high-dimensional systems and is suitable for real-time applications.

AI.tldr: CACTO-BIC improves upon CACTO by enhancing data efficiency through value-function-guided initial-state sampling and reducing computation time via GPU acceleration, enabling faster, scalable solutions for high-dimensional optimal control problems.
AI.motivation: Trajectory Optimization (TO) efficiently computes locally optimal solutions but struggles with non-convexity, while Reinforcement Learning (RL) is robust to non-convexity but computationally expensive. CACTO combines these strengths but faces scalability limitations as system complexity increases computational costs.
AI.method: CACTO-BIC enhances data efficiency by biasing initial-state sampling using value function properties of locally optimal policies, and reduces computation time by leveraging GPU acceleration for parallel processing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate improved sample efficiency and faster computation compared to CACTO, achieving solutions similar to PPO in less time. The approach scales to high-dimensional systems like the AlienGO quadruped robot and is suitable for real-time applications.
AI.conclusion: CACTO-BIC successfully addresses scalability challenges in hybrid TO-RL methods, enabling efficient, real-time optimal control on complex systems.

title: SAMAS: A Spectrum-Guided Multi-Agent System for Achieving Style Fidelity in Literary Translation
summary: Modern large language models (LLMs) excel at generating fluent and faithful translations. However, they struggle to preserve an author's unique literary style, often producing semantically correct but generic outputs. This limitation stems from the inability of current single-model and static multi-agent systems to perceive and adapt to stylistic variations. To address this, we introduce the Style-Adaptive Multi-Agent System (SAMAS), a novel framework that treats style preservation as a signal processing task. Specifically, our method quantifies literary style into a Stylistic Feature Spectrum (SFS) using the wavelet packet transform. This SFS serves as a control signal to dynamically assemble a tailored workflow of specialized translation agents based on the source text's structural patterns. Extensive experiments on translation benchmarks show that SAMAS achieves competitive semantic accuracy against strong baselines, primarily by leveraging its statistically significant advantage in style fidelity.

AI.tldr: A Style-Adaptive Multi-Agent System (SAMAS) that quantifies literary style into a Stylistic Feature Spectrum (SFS) using wavelet packet transform, and uses it to dynamically assemble specialized translation agent workflows for improved style preservation.
AI.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) can generate fluent and faithful translations but struggle to preserve an author's unique literary style, often producing generic outputs. This limitation stems from the inability of current single-model and static multi-agent systems to perceive and adapt to stylistic variations.
AI.method: Treats style preservation as a signal processing task. The method quantifies literary style into a Stylistic Feature Spectrum (SFS) using wavelet packet transform. This SFS serves as a control signal to dynamically assemble a tailored workflow of specialized translation agents based on the source text's structural patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on translation benchmarks show that SAMAS achieves competitive semantic accuracy against strong baselines, primarily by leveraging its statistically significant advantage in style fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The SAMAS framework effectively addresses the challenge of style preservation in translation, achieving better style fidelity while maintaining semantic accuracy through its adaptive multi-agent architecture.

title: FOCA: Frequency-Oriented Cross-Domain Forgery Detection, Localization and Explanation via Multi-Modal Large Language Model
summary: Advances in image tampering techniques, particularly generative models, pose significant challenges to media verification, digital forensics, and public trust. Existing image forgery detection and localization (IFDL) methods suffer from two key limitations: over-reliance on semantic content while neglecting textural cues, and limited interpretability of subtle low-level tampering traces. To address these issues, we propose FOCA, a multimodal large language model-based framework that integrates discriminative features from both the RGB spatial and frequency domains via a cross-attention fusion module. This design enables accurate forgery detection and localization while providing explicit, human-interpretable cross-domain explanations. We further introduce FSE-Set, a large-scale dataset with diverse authentic and tampered images, pixel-level masks, and dual-domain annotations. Extensive experiments show that FOCA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in detection performance and interpretability across both spatial and frequency domains.

AI.tldr: FOCA is a multimodal LLM framework that fuses RGB spatial and frequency domain features via cross-attention to detect and localize image tampering with interpretable explanations, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Image tampering detection suffers from over-reliance on semantic content while neglecting textural cues, and limited interpretability of subtle low-level tampering traces, creating challenges for media verification and digital forensics.
AI.method: FOCA employs a multimodal large language model with a cross-attention fusion module that integrates discriminative features from both RGB spatial and frequency domains, along with the FSE-Set dataset containing authentic/tampered images with pixel-level masks and dual-domain annotations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate FOCA achieves superior detection performance and interpretability compared to state-of-the-art methods across both spatial and frequency domains.
AI.conclusion: By combining dual-domain feature fusion with LLM-based interpretability, FOCA provides a more effective solution for image forgery detection and localization that balances technical accuracy with human-understandable explanations.

title: Classroom Final Exam: An Instructor-Tested Reasoning Benchmark
summary: We introduce \CFE{} (\textbf{C}lassroom \textbf{F}inal \textbf{E}xam), a multimodal benchmark for evaluating the reasoning capabilities of large language models across more than 20 STEM domains. \CFE{} is curated from repeatedly used, authentic university homework and exam problems, together with reference solutions provided by course instructors. \CFE{} presents a significant challenge even for frontier models: the newly released Gemini-3.1-pro-preview achieves an overall accuracy of 59.69\%, while the second-best model, Gemini-3-flash-preview, reaches 55.46\%, leaving considerable room for improvement. Beyond leaderboard results, we perform a diagnostic analysis by decomposing reference solutions into reasoning flows. We find that although frontier models can often answer intermediate sub-questions correctly, they struggle to reliably derive and maintain correct intermediate states throughout multi-step solutions. We further observe that model-generated solutions typically have more reasoning steps than those provided by the instructor, indicating suboptimal step efficiency and a higher risk of error accumulation. The data and code are available at https://github.com/Analogy-AI/CFE_Bench.

AI.tldr: CFE (Classroom Final Exam) is a multimodal benchmark for evaluating LLM reasoning across 20+ STEM domains, using authentic university problems with instructor solutions. Frontier models achieve ~59.69% accuracy, showing significant room for improvement in maintaining correct intermediate states and step efficiency.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for comprehensive, realistic benchmarks to evaluate large language model reasoning capabilities, particularly in STEM domains, by curating authentic academic problems that reflect real-world challenges faced by students and instructors.
AI.method: CFE is created by collecting repeatedly used, authentic university homework and exam problems across 20+ STEM domains, including instructor-provided reference solutions. The benchmark is evaluated using frontier models (Gemini-3.1-pro-preview and Gemini-3-flash-preview), with diagnostic analysis decomposing reference solutions into reasoning flows to understand model performance patterns.
AI.result: Frontier models achieve only ~59.69% overall accuracy on CFE (Gemini-3.1-pro-preview) with ~55.46% for the second-best model (Gemini-3-flash-preview). Diagnostic analysis reveals that while models correctly answer intermediate sub-questions, they struggle to maintain correct intermediate states throughout multi-step solutions and generate more reasoning steps than instructor solutions, leading to higher error accumulation.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that despite answering intermediate sub-questions correctly, frontier models have significant limitations in maintaining consistent reasoning states across complex multi-step solutions and lack the step efficiency of human instructors. This indicates substantial room for improvement in LLM reasoning capabilities for real-world academic tasks.

title: DeepInterestGR: Mining Deep Multi-Interest Using Multi-Modal LLMs for Generative Recommendation
summary: Recent generative recommendation frameworks have demonstrated remarkable scaling potential by reformulating item prediction as autoregressive Semantic ID (SID) generation. However, existing methods primarily rely on shallow behavioral signals, encoding items solely through surface-level textual features such as titles and descriptions. This reliance results in a critical Shallow Interest problem: the model fails to capture the latent, semantically rich interests underlying user interactions, limiting both personalization depth and recommendation interpretability. DeepInterestGR introduces three key innovations: (1) Multi-LLM Interest Mining (MLIM): We leverage multiple frontier LLMs along with their multi-modal variants to extract deep textual and visual interest representations through Chain-of-Thought prompting. (2) Reward-Labeled Deep Interest (RLDI): We employ a lightweight binary classifier to assign reward labels to mined interests, enabling effective supervision signals for reinforcement learning. (3) Interest-Enhanced Item Discretization (IEID): The curated deep interests are encoded into semantic embeddings and quantized into SID tokens via RQ-VAE. We adopt a two-stage training pipeline: supervised fine-tuning aligns the generative model with deep interest signals and collaborative filtering patterns, followed by reinforcement learning with GRPO optimized by our Interest-Aware Reward. Experiments on three Amazon Review benchmarks demonstrate that DeepInterestGR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across HR@K and NDCG@K metrics.

AI.tldr: DeepInterestGR is a generative recommendation framework that addresses the Shallow Interest problem by leveraging multiple LLMs to mine deep interests, using reward-labeled supervision, and encoding them into semantic ID tokens via RQ-VAE, achieving superior performance on Amazon Review benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing generative recommendation frameworks reformulate item prediction as autoregressive Semantic ID (SID) generation but rely on shallow behavioral signals (surface-level textual features like titles and descriptions). This results in the "Shallow Interest problem" where latent, semantically rich interests underlying user interactions are not captured, limiting personalization depth and recommendation interpretability.
AI.method: DeepInterestGR introduces three key innovations: (1) Multi-LLM Interest Mining (MLIM) using multiple frontier LLMs and their multi-modal variants with Chain-of-Thought prompting to extract deep textual and visual interest representations; (2) Reward-Labeled Deep Interest (RLDI) employing a lightweight binary classifier to assign reward labels to mined interests for RL supervision; (3) Interest-Enhanced Item Discretization (IEID) encoding curated deep interests into semantic embeddings and quantizing them into SID tokens via RQ-VAE. The framework adopts a two-stage training pipeline: supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with GRPO optimized by an Interest-Aware Reward.
AI.result: Experiments on three Amazon Review benchmarks demonstrate that DeepInterestGR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across HR@K and NDCG@K metrics.
AI.conclusion: DeepInterestGR effectively addresses the Shallow Interest problem by capturing latent, semantically rich user interests through multi-LLM interest mining, reward-labeled supervision, and interest-enhanced discretization, leading to improved personalization depth and recommendation interpretability.

title: Towards Dexterous Embodied Manipulation via Deep Multi-Sensory Fusion and Sparse Expert Scaling
summary: Realizing dexterous embodied manipulation necessitates the deep integration of heterogeneous multimodal sensory inputs. However, current vision-centric paradigms often overlook the critical force and geometric feedback essential for complex tasks. This paper presents DeMUSE, a Deep Multimodal Unified Sparse Experts framework leveraging a Diffusion Transformer to integrate RGB, depth, and 6-axis force into a unified serialized stream. Adaptive Modality-specific Normalization (AdaMN) is employed to recalibrate modality-aware features, mitigating representation imbalance and harmonizing the heterogeneous distributions of multi-sensory signals. To facilitate efficient scaling, the architecture utilizes a Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with shared experts, increasing model capacity for physical priors while maintaining the low inference latency required for real-time control. A Joint denoising objective synchronously synthesizes environmental evolution and action sequences to ensure physical consistency. Achieving success rates of 83.2% and 72.5% in simulation and real-world trials, DeMUSE demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, validating the necessity of deep multi-sensory integration for complex physical interactions.

AI.tldr: DeMUSE is a Deep Multimodal Unified Sparse Experts framework that integrates RGB, depth, and 6-axis force data using a Diffusion Transformer with Adaptive Modality-specific Normalization and Sparse Mixture-of-Experts architecture to enable dexterous embodied manipulation.
AI.motivation: Dexterous embodied manipulation requires deep integration of heterogeneous multimodal sensory inputs, but current vision-centric paradigms overlook critical force and geometric feedback essential for complex tasks.
AI.method: DeMUSE leverages a Diffusion Transformer to integrate RGB, depth, and 6-axis force into a unified serialized stream. It employs Adaptive Modality-specific Normalization (AdaMN) to recalibrate modality-aware features and Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with shared experts for efficient scaling. A Joint denoising objective synchronizes environmental evolution and action sequences to ensure physical consistency.
AI.result: Achieved 83.2% success rate in simulation and 72.5% success rate in real-world trials, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: The results validate the necessity of deep multi-sensory integration for complex physical interactions in dexterous embodied manipulation tasks.

title: SHIELD: Semantic Heterogeneity Integrated Embedding for Latent Discovery in Clinical Trial Safety Signals
summary: We present SHIELD, a novel methodology for automated and integrated safety signal detection in clinical trials. SHIELD combines disproportionality analysis with semantic clustering of adverse event (AE) terms applied to MedDRA term embeddings. For each AE, the pipeline computes an information-theoretic disproportionality measure (Information Component) with effect size derived via empirical Bayesian shrinkage. A utility matrix is constructed by weighting semantic term-term similarities by signal magnitude, followed by spectral embedding and clustering to identify groups of related AEs. Resulting clusters are annotated with syndrome-level summary labels using large language models, yielding a coherent, data-driven representation of treatment-associated safety profiles in the form of a network graph and hierarchical tree. We implement the SHIELD framework in the context of a single-arm incidence summary, to compare two treatment arms or for the detection of any treatment effect in a multi-arm trial. We illustrate its ability to recover known safety signals and generate interpretable, cluster-based summaries in a real clinical trial example. This work bridges statistical signal detection with modern natural language processing to enhance safety assessment and causal interpretation in clinical trials.

AI.tldr: SHIELD is a novel methodology for automated safety signal detection in clinical trials that combines disproportionality analysis with semantic clustering of adverse event terms using MedDRA term embeddings and large language models.
AI.motivation: The work aims to bridge statistical signal detection with modern natural language processing to enhance safety assessment and causal interpretation in clinical trials, providing interpretable, cluster-based summaries of treatment-associated safety profiles.
AI.method: SHIELD computes an information-theoretic disproportionality measure (Information Component) with effect size via empirical Bayesian shrinkage, constructs a utility matrix by weighting semantic term-term similarities by signal magnitude, uses spectral embedding and clustering to identify related AE groups, and annotates clusters with syndrome-level labels using large language models.
AI.result: The framework successfully recovered known safety signals and generated interpretable, cluster-based summaries in a real clinical trial example, providing a coherent, data-driven representation of treatment-associated safety profiles as network graphs and hierarchical trees.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully bridges statistical signal detection with NLP techniques to enhance safety assessment and causal interpretation in clinical trials, demonstrating practical utility through recovery of known signals and generation of interpretable summaries.

title: SceneTok: A Compressed, Diffusable Token Space for 3D Scenes
summary: We present SceneTok, a novel tokenizer for encoding view sets of scenes into a compressed and diffusable set of unstructured tokens. Existing approaches for 3D scene representation and generation commonly use 3D data structures or view-aligned fields. In contrast, we introduce the first method that encodes scene information into a small set of permutation-invariant tokens that is disentangled from the spatial grid. The scene tokens are predicted by a multi-view tokenizer given many context views and rendered into novel views by employing a light-weight rectified flow decoder. We show that the compression is 1-3 orders of magnitude stronger than for other representations while still reaching state-of-the-art reconstruction quality. Further, our representation can be rendered from novel trajectories, including ones deviating from the input trajectory, and we show that the decoder gracefully handles uncertainty. Finally, the highly-compressed set of unstructured latent scene tokens enables simple and efficient scene generation in 5 seconds, achieving a much better quality-speed trade-off than previous paradigms.

AI.tldr: SceneTok is a novel tokenizer that encodes 3D scene view sets into a small set of permutation-invariant, spatially-disentangled tokens, achieving 1-3 orders of magnitude stronger compression than existing representations while maintaining state-of-the-art quality and enabling efficient novel view rendering.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D scene representation and generation methods rely on 3D data structures or view-aligned fields, which limit flexibility in handling novel trajectories and uncertainty. There is a need for a more compact and flexible representation that can be efficiently rendered from arbitrary viewpoints.
AI.method: SceneTok uses a multi-view tokenizer to predict a small set of permutation-invariant tokens from context views, which are then rendered into novel views using a lightweight rectified flow decoder. The tokens are disentangled from the spatial grid, allowing for efficient compression and flexible rendering.
AI.result: SceneTok achieves 1-3 orders of magnitude stronger compression than other representations while maintaining state-of-the-art reconstruction quality. It enables rendering from novel trajectories (including those deviating from input trajectories) with graceful handling of uncertainty. Scene generation can be performed in just 5 seconds with a better quality-speed trade-off than previous paradigms.
AI.conclusion: SceneTok represents a significant advance in 3D scene representation by introducing a highly compressed, permutation-invariant token representation that maintains high-quality rendering from novel viewpoints. This approach offers a substantial improvement in compression efficiency and rendering flexibility, with practical benefits for real-time scene generation applications.

title: Ada-RS: Adaptive Rejection Sampling for Selective Thinking
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed in cost and latency-sensitive settings. While chain-of-thought improves reasoning, it can waste tokens on simple requests. We study selective thinking for tool-using LLMs and introduce Adaptive Rejection Sampling (Ada-RS), an algorithm-agnostic sample filtering framework for learning selective and efficient reasoning. For each given context, Ada-RS scores multiple sampled completions with an adaptive length-penalized reward then applies stochastic rejection sampling to retain only high-reward candidates (or preference pairs) for downstream optimization. We demonstrate how Ada-RS plugs into both preference pair (e.g. DPO) or grouped policy optimization strategies (e.g. DAPO). Using Qwen3-8B with LoRA on a synthetic tool call-oriented e-commerce benchmark, Ada-RS improves the accuracy-efficiency frontier over standard algorithms by reducing average output tokens by up to 80% and reducing thinking rate by up to 95% while maintaining or improving tool call accuracy. These results highlight that training-signal selection is a powerful lever for efficient reasoning in latency-sensitive deployments.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Rejection Sampling (Ada-RS) is a sample filtering framework that enables tool-using LLMs to selectively apply chain-of-thought reasoning, reducing token usage by up to 80% while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in cost and latency-sensitive settings waste tokens on simple requests when using chain-of-thought, creating a need for selective reasoning that balances accuracy with efficiency.
AI.method: Ada-RS scores multiple sampled completions using an adaptive length-penalized reward function, then applies stochastic rejection sampling to retain high-reward candidates for downstream optimization. It integrates with preference pair methods like DPO and grouped policy optimization like DAPO.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B with LoRA on an e-commerce benchmark, Ada-RS reduces average output tokens by up to 80% and thinking rate by up to 95% while maintaining or improving tool call accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Training-signal selection is a powerful lever for efficient reasoning in latency-sensitive deployments, demonstrating that Ada-RS effectively balances accuracy and efficiency through adaptive sample filtering.

title: SLDP: Semi-Local Differential Privacy for Density-Adaptive Analytics
summary: Density-adaptive domain discretization is essential for high-utility privacy-preserving analytics but remains challenging under Local Differential Privacy (LDP) due to the privacy-budget costs associated with iterative refinement. We propose a novel framework, Semi-Local Differential Privacy (SLDP), that assigns a privacy region to each user based on local density and defines adjacency by the potential movement of a point within its privacy region. We present an interactive $(\varepsilon, δ)$-SLDP protocol, orchestrated by an honest-but-curious server over a public channel, to estimate these regions privately. Crucially, our framework decouples the privacy cost from the number of refinement iterations, allowing for high-resolution grids without additional privacy budget cost. We experimentally demonstrate the framework's effectiveness on estimation tasks across synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: A novel Semi-Local Differential Privacy (SLDP) framework that assigns privacy regions based on local density, enabling high-resolution domain discretization without additional privacy budget costs under Local Differential Privacy.
AI.motivation: Density-adaptive domain discretization is essential for high-utility privacy-preserving analytics but remains challenging under Local Differential Privacy (LDP) due to privacy-budget costs associated with iterative refinement processes.
AI.method: The authors propose Semi-Local Differential Privacy (SLDP), which assigns a privacy region to each user based on local density and defines adjacency by potential point movement within these regions. They introduce an interactive (ε, δ)-SLDP protocol orchestrated by an honest-but-curious server over a public channel to privately estimate these privacy regions.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness on estimation tasks across both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The key contribution is the decoupling of privacy cost from the number of refinement iterations, allowing for high-resolution grids without additional privacy budget consumption, which addresses the main challenge in density-adaptive discretization under LDP.

title: TactiVerse: Generalizing Multi-Point Tactile Sensing in Soft Robotics Using Single-Point Data
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, accepted at 9th IEEE-RAS International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
summary: Real-time prediction of deformation in highly compliant soft materials remains a significant challenge in soft robotics. While vision-based soft tactile sensors can track internal marker displacements, learning-based models for 3D contact estimation heavily depend on their training datasets, inherently limiting their ability to generalize to complex scenarios such as multi-point sensing. To address this limitation, we introduce TactiVerse, a U-Net-based framework that formulates contact geometry estimation as a spatial heatmap prediction task. Even when trained exclusively on a limited dataset of single-point indentations, our architecture achieves highly accurate single-point sensing, yielding a superior mean absolute error of 0.0589 mm compared to the 0.0612 mm of a conventional regression-based CNN baseline. Furthermore, we demonstrate that augmenting the training dataset with multi-point contact data substantially enhances the sensor's multi-point sensing capabilities, significantly improving the overall mean MAE for two-point discrimination from 1.214 mm to 0.383 mm. By successfully extrapolating complex contact geometries from fundamental interactions, this methodology unlocks advanced multi-point and large-area shape sensing. Ultimately, it significantly streamlines the development of marker-based soft sensors, offering a highly scalable solution for real-world tactile perception.

AI.tldr: TactiVerse is a U-Net-based framework that formulates contact geometry estimation as a spatial heatmap prediction task, enabling highly accurate single-point and multi-point deformation sensing in soft tactile sensors.
AI.motivation: Real-time prediction of deformation in highly compliant soft materials is challenging. Vision-based soft tactile sensors can track internal marker displacements, but learning-based 3D contact estimation models heavily depend on training datasets and struggle to generalize to complex scenarios like multi-point sensing.
AI.method: TactiVerse adopts a U-Net architecture that reformulates contact geometry estimation as a spatial heatmap prediction task. The framework was trained on single-point indentation data and demonstrated enhanced performance when augmented with multi-point contact data.
AI.result: Even with single-point indentation training only, TactiVerse achieved a superior mean absolute error of 0.0589 mm compared to 0.0612 mm for a conventional regression-based CNN baseline. When augmented with multi-point data, two-point discrimination MAE improved from 1.214 mm to 0.383 mm.
AI.conclusion: The methodology successfully extrapolates complex contact geometries from fundamental interactions, unlocking advanced multi-point and large-area shape sensing capabilities. It streamlines marker-based soft sensor development and offers a highly scalable solution for real-world tactile perception.

title: A Multimodal Framework for Aligning Human Linguistic Descriptions with Visual Perceptual Data
summary: Establishing stable mappings between natural language expressions and visual percepts is a foundational problem for both cognitive science and artificial intelligence. Humans routinely ground linguistic reference in noisy, ambiguous perceptual contexts, yet the mechanisms supporting such cross-modal alignment remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a computational framework designed to model core aspects of human referential interpretation by integrating linguistic utterances with perceptual representations derived from large-scale, crowd-sourced imagery. The system approximates human perceptual categorization by combining scale-invariant feature transform (SIFT) alignment with the Universal Quality Index (UQI) to quantify similarity in a cognitively plausible feature space, while a set of linguistic preprocessing and query-transformation operations captures pragmatic variability in referring expressions. We evaluate the model on the Stanford Repeated Reference Game corpus (15,000 utterances paired with tangram stimuli), a paradigm explicitly developed to probe human-level perceptual ambiguity and coordination. Our framework achieves robust referential grounding. It requires 65\% fewer utterances than human interlocutors to reach stable mappings and can correctly identify target objects from single referring expressions 41.66\% of the time (versus 20\% for humans).These results suggest that relatively simple perceptual-linguistic alignment mechanisms can yield human-competitive behavior on a classic cognitive benchmark, and offers insights into models of grounded communication, perceptual inference, and cross-modal concept formation. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/metasequoia-9D13/README.md .

AI.tldr: A computational framework for modeling human referential interpretation by integrating linguistic utterances with perceptual representations, achieving human-competitive performance on referential grounding tasks.
AI.motivation: Establishing stable mappings between natural language expressions and visual percepts is a foundational problem in cognitive science and AI. Humans routinely ground linguistic reference in noisy, ambiguous perceptual contexts, yet the mechanisms supporting such cross-modal alignment remain poorly understood.
AI.method: The framework combines scale-invariant feature transform (SIFT) alignment with the Universal Quality Index (UQI) for perceptual similarity quantification in a cognitively plausible feature space, enhanced by linguistic preprocessing and query-transformation operations to capture pragmatic variability. Evaluated on the Stanford Repeated Reference Game corpus (15,000 utterances paired with tangram stimuli).
AI.result: Achieves robust referential grounding: requires 65% fewer utterances than humans to reach stable mappings and correctly identifies target objects from single referring expressions 41.66% of the time (versus 20% for humans).
AI.conclusion: Relatively simple perceptual-linguistic alignment mechanisms can yield human-competitive behavior on classic cognitive benchmarks, offering insights into grounded communication, perceptual inference, and cross-modal concept formation models.

title: From Human-Level AI Tales to AI Leveling Human Scales
summary: Comparing AI models to "human level" is often misleading when benchmark scores are incommensurate or human baselines are drawn from a narrow population. To address this, we propose a framework that calibrates items against the 'world population' and report performance on a common, human-anchored scale. Concretely, we build on a set of multi-level scales for different capabilities where each level should represent a probability of success of the whole world population on a logarithmic scale with a base $B$. We calibrate each scale for each capability (reasoning, comprehension, knowledge, volume, etc.) by compiling publicly released human test data spanning education and reasoning benchmarks (PISA, TIMSS, ICAR, UKBioBank, and ReliabilityBench). The base $B$ is estimated by extrapolating between samples with two demographic profiles using LLMs, with the hypothesis that they condense rich information about human populations. We evaluate the quality of different mappings using group slicing and post-stratification. The new techniques allow for the recalibration and standardization of scales relative to the whole-world population.

AI.tldr: A framework is proposed to calibrate AI models against the "world population" using multi-level scales where each level represents a probability of success on a logarithmic scale, enabling fair comparison of AI performance to human capability.
AI.motivation: Comparing AI models to human level is misleading when benchmark scores are incommensurate or human baselines are drawn from narrow populations. A more accurate calibration against the whole-world population is needed.
AI.method: Builds on multi-level scales for different capabilities where each level represents a probability of success on a logarithmic scale with base B. Calibrates scales using publicly released human test data (PISA, TIMSS, ICAR, UKBioBank, ReliabilityBench). Estimates base B by extrapolating between samples with two demographic profiles using LLMs. Evaluates mapping quality using group slicing and post-stratification.
AI.result: The new techniques enable recalibration and standardization of scales relative to the whole-world population, providing a common, human-anchored scale for performance comparison.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework allows for more accurate and fair comparison of AI models to human performance by anchoring to the world population rather than narrow or incommensurate baselines.

title: Athena: An Autonomous Open-Hardware Tracked Rescue Robot Platform
summary: In disaster response and situation assessment, robots have great potential in reducing the risks to the safety and health of first responders. As the situations encountered and the required capabilities of the robots deployed in such missions differ wildly and are often not known in advance, heterogeneous fleets of robots are needed to cover a wide range of mission requirements. While UAVs can quickly survey the mission environment, their ability to carry heavy payloads such as sensors and manipulators is limited. UGVs can carry required payloads to assess and manipulate the mission environment, but need to be able to deal with difficult and unstructured terrain such as rubble and stairs. The ability of tracked platforms with articulated arms (flippers) to reconfigure their geometry makes them particularly effective for navigating challenging terrain. In this paper, we present Athena, an open-hardware rescue ground robot research platform with four individually reconfigurable flippers and a reliable low-cost remote emergency stop (E-Stop) solution. A novel mounting solution using an industrial PU belt and tooth inserts allows the replacement and testing of different track profiles. The manipulator with a maximum reach of 1.54m can be used to operate doors, valves, and other objects of interest. Full CAD & PCB files, as well as all low-level software, are released as open-source contributions.

AI.tldr: Athena is an open-hardware rescue ground robot platform featuring four individually reconfigurable flippers for terrain navigation, a 1.54m reach manipulator, and a low-cost remote emergency stop solution, with all design files and software released as open-source.
AI.motivation: Disaster response requires heterogeneous robot fleets due to unpredictable environments and varied mission requirements. UAVs cannot carry heavy payloads while UGVs struggle with difficult terrain, making tracked platforms with articulated arms particularly effective for rescue missions.
AI.method: The paper introduces Athena, a rescue ground robot with four individually reconfigurable flippers, a novel mounting solution using industrial PU belt and tooth inserts for track profile replacement, and a reliable low-cost remote emergency stop. The manipulator has a maximum reach of 1.54m for operating doors, valves, and other objects. Full CAD, PCB files, and low-level software are open-sourced.
AI.result: Athena demonstrates a functional rescue robot platform with reconfigurable flippers for challenging terrain navigation, payload carrying capabilities, and emergency stop functionality. The platform is fully documented with all design files and software released as open-source contributions.
AI.conclusion: By releasing Athena as an open-source platform, the authors provide the research community with a foundation for further development of rescue robots capable of handling diverse mission requirements in unstructured disaster environments.

title: SafeDrive: Fine-Grained Safety Reasoning for End-to-End Driving in a Sparse World
summary: The end-to-end (E2E) paradigm, which maps sensor inputs directly to driving decisions, has recently attracted significant attention due to its unified modeling capability and scalability. However, ensuring safety in this unified framework remains one of the most critical challenges. In this work, we propose SafeDrive, an E2E planning framework designed to perform explicit and interpretable safety reasoning through a trajectory-conditioned Sparse World Model. SafeDrive comprises two complementary networks: the Sparse World Network (SWNet) and the Fine-grained Reasoning Network (FRNet). SWNet constructs trajectory-conditioned sparse worlds that simulate the future behaviors of critical dynamic agents and road entities, providing interaction-centric representations for downstream reasoning. FRNet then evaluates agent-specific collision risks and temporal adherence to drivable regions, enabling precise identification of safety-critical events across future timesteps. SafeDrive achieves state-of-the-art performance on both open-loop and closed-loop benchmarks. On NAVSIM, it records a PDMS of 91.6 and an EPDMS of 87.5, with only 61 collisions out of 12,146 scenarios (0.5%). On Bench2Drive, SafeDrive attains a 66.8% driving score.

AI.tldr: SafeDrive is an end-to-end autonomous driving planning framework that introduces explicit and interpretable safety reasoning through a trajectory-conditioned Sparse World Model (SWNet) and Fine-grained Reasoning Network (FRNet), achieving state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM (PDMS 91.6, EPDMS 87.5, 0.5% collision rate) and Bench2Drive (66.8% driving score).
AI.motivation: End-to-end (E2E) paradigms for autonomous driving offer unified modeling and scalability but face critical safety challenges. Ensuring safety within the unified framework remains one of the most difficult problems to solve in autonomous driving research.
AI.method: SafeDrive comprises two complementary networks: (1) Sparse World Network (SWNet) constructs trajectory-conditioned sparse worlds simulating future behaviors of critical dynamic agents and road entities, providing interaction-centric representations; (2) Fine-grained Reasoning Network (FRNet) evaluates agent-specific collision risks and temporal adherence to drivable regions, identifying safety-critical events across future timesteps.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on both open-loop and closed-loop benchmarks: NAVSIM (PDMS 91.6, EPDMS 87.5, 61 collisions out of 12,146 scenarios = 0.5%); Bench2Drive (66.8% driving score).
AI.conclusion: SafeDrive successfully addresses the safety challenge in E2E autonomous driving by introducing explicit and interpretable safety reasoning mechanisms. The framework demonstrates that safety can be integrated into end-to-end planning while maintaining high performance, offering a promising approach for deploying safe autonomous vehicles.

title: Rules or Weights? Comparing User Understanding of Explainable AI Techniques with the Cognitive XAI-Adaptive Model
summary: Rules and Weights are popular XAI techniques for explaining AI decisions. Yet, it remains unclear how to choose between them, lacking a cognitive framework to compare their interpretability. In an elicitation user study on forward and counterfactual decision tasks, we identified 7 reasoning strategies of interpreting three XAI Schemas - weights, rules, and their hybrid. To analyze their capabilities, we propose CoXAM, a Cognitive XAI-Adaptive Model with shared memory representation to encode instance attributes, linear weights, and decision rules. CoXAM employs computational rationality to choose among reasoning processes based on the trade-off in utility and reasoning time, separately for forward or counterfactual decision tasks. In a validation study, CoXAM demonstrated a stronger alignment with human decision-making compared to baseline machine learning proxy models. The model successfully replicated and explained several key empirical findings, including that counterfactual tasks are inherently harder than forward tasks, decision tree rules are harder to recall and apply than linear weights, and the helpfulness of XAI depends on the application data context, alongside identifying which underlying reasoning strategies were most effective. With CoXAM, we contribute a cognitive basis to accelerate debugging and benchmarking disparate XAI techniques.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CoXAM, a cognitive XAI-adaptive model that compares interpretability between weights, rules, and hybrid explanations by analyzing human reasoning strategies in decision tasks. The model demonstrates stronger alignment with human decision-making than baselines and validates empirical findings about XAI effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Rules and weights are popular XAI techniques, but there is no cognitive framework to compare their interpretability or guide selection between them. The lack of a cognitive basis makes it unclear how to choose between these explanations.
AI.method: The authors conducted an elicitation user study on forward and counterfactual decision tasks to identify 7 reasoning strategies for interpreting weights, rules, and hybrid schemas. They proposed CoXAM, a Cognitive XAI-Adaptive Model with shared memory representation encoding instance attributes, linear weights, and decision rules. CoXAM employs computational rationality to choose reasoning processes based on utility and reasoning time trade-offs. Validation compared CoXAM against baseline machine learning proxy models.
AI.result: CoXAM demonstrated stronger alignment with human decision-making than baseline models. It successfully replicated key empirical findings: counterfactual tasks are inherently harder than forward tasks, decision tree rules are harder to recall and apply than linear weights, XAI helpfulness depends on application data context, and identified which underlying reasoning strategies were most effective.
AI.conclusion: CoXAM provides a cognitive basis for accelerating debugging and benchmarking disparate XAI techniques, enabling more informed selection between rules, weights, and hybrid explanations based on their cognitive characteristics and task requirements.

title: LoMime: Query-Efficient Membership Inference using Model Extraction in Label-Only Settings
summary: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) threaten the privacy of machine learning models by revealing whether a specific data point was used during training. Existing MIAs often rely on impractical assumptions such as access to public datasets, shadow models, confidence scores, or training data distribution knowledge and making them vulnerable to defenses like confidence masking and adversarial regularization. Label-only MIAs, even under strict constraints suffer from high query requirements per sample. We propose a cost-effective label-only MIA framework based on transferability and model extraction. By querying the target model M using active sampling, perturbation-based selection, and synthetic data, we extract a functionally similar surrogate S on which membership inference is performed. This shifts query overhead to a one-time extraction phase, eliminating repeated queries to M . Operating under strict black-box constraints, our method matches the performance of state-of-the-art label-only MIAs while significantly reducing query costs. On benchmarks including Purchase, Location, and Texas Hospital, we show that a query budget equivalent to testing $\approx1\%$ of training samples suffices to extract S and achieve membership inference accuracy within $\pm1\%$ of M . We also evaluate the effectiveness of standard defenses proposed for label-only MIAs against our attack.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cost-effective label-only membership inference attack (MIA) that reduces query costs by first extracting a functionally similar surrogate model and then performing membership inference on the surrogate instead of repeatedly querying the target model.
AI.motivation: Existing MIAs suffer from limitations: they rely on impractical assumptions (public datasets, shadow models, confidence scores, training data distribution knowledge), are vulnerable to defenses like confidence masking and adversarial regularization, and require high query requirements per sample even under strict black-box constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose a label-only MIA framework based on transferability and model extraction. The method uses active sampling, perturbation-based selection, and synthetic data to query the target model M and extract a functionally similar surrogate model S. Membership inference is then performed on S, shifting query overhead to a one-time extraction phase.
AI.result: On benchmarks including Purchase, Location, and Texas Hospital datasets, a query budget equivalent to testing approximately 1% of training samples suffices to extract S and achieve membership inference accuracy within ±1% of M. The method matches state-of-the-art label-only MIAs while significantly reducing query costs.
AI.conclusion: Operating under strict black-box constraints, the proposed method effectively bypasses standard defenses proposed for label-only MIAs. By shifting query overhead to a one-time extraction phase, it achieves comparable membership inference performance with dramatically reduced query requirements, making it a practical and effective attack strategy.

title: Scaling Law of Neural Koopman Operators
summary: Data-driven neural Koopman operator theory has emerged as a powerful tool for linearizing and controlling nonlinear robotic systems. However, the performance of these data-driven models fundamentally depends on the trade-off between sample size and model dimensions, a relationship for which the scaling laws have remained unclear. This paper establishes a rigorous framework to address this challenge by deriving and empirically validating scaling laws that connect sample size, latent space dimension, and downstream control quality. We derive a theoretical upper bound on the Koopman approximation error, explicitly decomposing it into sampling error and projection error. We show that these terms decay at specific rates relative to dataset size and latent dimension, providing a rigorous basis for the scaling law. Based on the theoretical results, we introduce two lightweight regularizers for the neural Koopman operator: a covariance loss to help stabilize the learned latent features and an inverse control loss to ensure the model aligns with physical actuation. The results from systematic experiments across six robotic environments confirm that model fitting error follows the derived scaling laws, and the regularizers improve dynamic model fitting fidelity, with enhanced closed-loop control performance. Together, our results provide a simple recipe for allocating effort between data collection and model capacity when learning Koopman dynamics for control.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes scaling laws for data-driven neural Koopman operators that connect sample size, latent space dimension, and control quality. It derives theoretical bounds on approximation error (decomposed into sampling and projection error) and introduces two regularizers (covariance loss and inverse control loss) that improve model fitting and closed-loop control performance across six robotic environments.
AI.motivation: Data-driven neural Koopman operators for nonlinear robotic systems face a fundamental trade-off between sample size and model dimensions, but the scaling laws governing this relationship remained unclear.
AI.method: The paper derives a theoretical framework establishing an upper bound on Koopman approximation error explicitly decomposed into sampling error and projection error. It introduces two lightweight regularizers: a covariance loss to stabilize learned latent features and an inverse control loss to align the model with physical actuation.
AI.result: Systematic experiments across six robotic environments confirm that model fitting error follows the derived scaling laws. The regularizers improve dynamic model fitting fidelity and enhance closed-loop control performance.
AI.conclusion: The results provide a practical recipe for allocating effort between data collection and model capacity when learning Koopman dynamics for control.

title: Janus-Q: End-to-End Event-Driven Trading via Hierarchical-Gated Reward Modeling
summary: Financial market movements are often driven by discrete financial events conveyed through news, whose impacts are heterogeneous, abrupt, and difficult to capture under purely numerical prediction objectives. These limitations have motivated growing interest in using textual information as the primary source of trading signals in learning-based systems. Two key challenges hinder existing approaches: (1) the absence of large-scale, event-centric datasets that jointly model news semantics and statistically grounded market reactions, and (2) the misalignment between language model reasoning and financially valid trading behavior under dynamic market conditions. To address these challenges, we propose Janus-Q, an end-to-end event-driven trading framework that elevates financial news events from auxiliary signals to primary decision units. Janus-Q unifies event-centric data construction and model optimization under a two-stage paradigm. Stage I focuses on event-centric data construction, building a large-scale financial news event dataset comprising 62,400 articles annotated with 10 fine-grained event types, associated stocks, sentiment labels, and event-driven cumulative abnormal return (CAR). Stage II performs decision-oriented fine-tuning, combining supervised learning with reinforcement learning guided by a Hierarchical Gated Reward Model (HGRM), which explicitly captures trade-offs among multiple trading objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Janus-Q achieves more consistent, interpretable, and profitable trading decisions than market indices and LLM baselines, improving the Sharpe Ratio by up to 102.0% while increasing direction accuracy by over 17.5% compared to the strongest competing strategies.

AI.tldr: Janus-Q is an end-to-end event-driven trading framework that constructs a large-scale financial news event dataset and uses reinforcement learning with hierarchical reward modeling to generate more profitable trading decisions than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Financial markets are driven by discrete events in news, but existing approaches face two key challenges: (1) lack of large-scale event-centric datasets that jointly model news semantics and statistically grounded market reactions; and (2) misalignment between language model reasoning and financially valid trading behavior under dynamic market conditions.
AI.method: Janus-Q employs a two-stage paradigm: Stage I constructs a large-scale financial news event dataset with 62,400 articles annotated with 10 fine-grained event types, stocks, sentiment labels, and event-driven cumulative abnormal returns (CAR). Stage II performs decision-oriented fine-tuning combining supervised learning with reinforcement learning guided by a Hierarchical Gated Reward Model (HGRM) that explicitly captures trade-offs among multiple trading objectives.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate Janus-Q achieves more consistent, interpretable, and profitable trading decisions than market indices and LLM baselines, improving the Sharpe Ratio by up to 102.0% and increasing direction accuracy by over 17.5% compared to the strongest competing strategies.
AI.conclusion: Janus-Q successfully elevates financial news events from auxiliary signals to primary decision units, demonstrating that event-centric data construction combined with hierarchical reward modeling can effectively bridge the gap between language model reasoning and financially valid trading behavior, leading to substantially improved risk-adjusted returns and trading accuracy.

title: Exponential Convergence of (Stochastic) Gradient Descent for Separable Logistic Regression
summary: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent are central to modern machine learning, yet their behavior under large step sizes remains theoretically unclear. Recent work suggests that acceleration often arises near the edge of stability, where optimization trajectories become unstable and difficult to analyze. Existing results for separable logistic regression achieve faster convergence by explicitly leveraging such unstable regimes through constant or adaptive large step sizes. In this paper, we show that instability is not inherent to acceleration. We prove that gradient descent with a simple, non-adaptive increasing step-size schedule achieves exponential convergence for separable logistic regression under a margin condition, while remaining entirely within a stable optimization regime. The resulting method is anytime and does not require prior knowledge of the optimization horizon or target accuracy. We also establish exponential convergence of stochastic gradient descent using a lightweight adaptive step-size rule that avoids line search and specialized procedures, improving upon existing polynomial-rate guarantees. Together, our results demonstrate that carefully structured step-size growth alone suffices to obtain exponential acceleration for both gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that carefully structured increasing step-size schedules can achieve exponential convergence for gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent on separable logistic regression while remaining in stable optimization regimes, without requiring instability or prior knowledge of optimization parameters.
AI.motivation: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent are central to ML, but their behavior under large step sizes remains unclear. Recent work suggests acceleration occurs near the edge of stability (unstable regimes), but this paper challenges the necessity of instability for acceleration.
AI.method: The authors prove exponential convergence results using two approaches: (1) gradient descent with a simple, non-adaptive increasing step-size schedule that achieves exponential convergence under a margin condition without prior knowledge; (2) stochastic gradient descent with a lightweight adaptive step-size rule that avoids line search and specialized procedures.
AI.result: Both methods achieve exponential convergence for separable logistic regression under a margin condition while remaining entirely within stable regimes. The SGD method improves upon existing polynomial-rate guarantees. The gradient descent method is "anytime" and does not require prior knowledge of the optimization horizon or target accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Carefully structured step-size growth alone suffices to obtain exponential acceleration for both gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent, demonstrating that instability is not inherent to acceleration.

title: Contextual Safety Reasoning and Grounding for Open-World Robots
summary: Robots are increasingly operating in open-world environments where safe behavior depends on context: the same hallway may require different navigation strategies when crowded versus empty, or during an emergency versus normal operations. Traditional safety approaches enforce fixed constraints in user-specified contexts, limiting their ability to handle the open-ended contextual variability of real-world deployment. We address this gap via CORE, a safety framework that enables online contextual reasoning, grounding, and enforcement without prior knowledge of the environment (e.g., maps or safety specifications). CORE uses a vision-language model (VLM) to continuously reason about context-dependent safety rules directly from visual observations, grounds these rules in the physical environment, and enforces the resulting spatially-defined safe sets via control barrier functions. We provide probabilistic safety guarantees for CORE that account for perceptual uncertainty, and we demonstrate through simulation and real-world experiments that CORE enforces contextually appropriate behavior in unseen environments, significantly outperforming prior semantic safety methods that lack online contextual reasoning. Ablation studies validate our theoretical guarantees and underscore the importance of both VLM-based reasoning and spatial grounding for enforcing contextual safety in novel settings. We provide additional resources at https://zacravichandran.github.io/CORE.

AI.tldr: CORE is a safety framework that enables online contextual reasoning and enforcement of safety rules for robots using vision-language models (VLMs) without prior knowledge of the environment, grounding rules physically via control barrier functions, and providing probabilistic safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in open-world environments require context-dependent safety behavior (e.g., different navigation strategies for crowded vs. empty hallways, emergency vs. normal operations). Traditional safety approaches with fixed constraints cannot handle the variability of real-world deployment contexts.
AI.method: CORE uses a vision-language model to continuously reason about context-dependent safety rules directly from visual observations, grounds these rules in the physical environment, and enforces resulting spatially-defined safe sets via control barrier functions. The framework operates without prior knowledge of the environment (e.g., maps or safety specifications).
AI.result: CORE achieves probabilistic safety guarantees accounting for perceptual uncertainty. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that CORE enforces contextually appropriate behavior in unseen environments, significantly outperforming prior semantic safety methods lacking online contextual reasoning. Ablation studies validate the theoretical guarantees and highlight the importance of VLM-based reasoning and spatial grounding.
AI.conclusion: Contextual safety enforcement in novel environments requires both vision-language model-based reasoning and spatial grounding. CORE successfully addresses this challenge without prior environmental knowledge, providing a robust framework for real-world robot deployment.

title: SCHEMA for Gemini 3 Pro Image: A Structured Methodology for Controlled AI Image Generation on Google's Native Multimodal Model
comment: 24 pages, 8 tables. Based on SCHEMA Method v1.0 (deposited December 11, 2025). Previously published on Zenodo: doi:10.5281/zenodo.18721380
summary: This paper presents SCHEMA (Structured Components for Harmonized Engineered Modular Architecture), a structured prompt engineering methodology specifically developed for Google Gemini 3 Pro Image. Unlike generic prompt guidelines or model-agnostic tips, SCHEMA is an engineered framework built on systematic professional practice encompassing 850 verified API predictions within an estimated corpus of approximately 4,800 generated images, spanning six professional domains: real estate photography, commercial product photography, editorial content, storyboards, commercial campaigns, and information design. The methodology introduces a three-tier progressive system (BASE, MEDIO, AVANZATO) that scales practitioner control from exploratory (approximately 5%) to directive (approximately 95%), a modular label architecture with 7 core and 5 optional structured components, a decision tree with explicit routing rules to alternative tools, and systematically documented model limitations with corresponding workarounds. Key findings include an observed 91% Mandatory compliance rate and 94% Prohibitions compliance rate across 621 structured prompts, a comparative batch consistency test demonstrating substantially higher inter-generation coherence for structured prompts, independent practitioner validation (n=40), and a dedicated Information Design validation demonstrating >95% first-generation compliance for spatial and typographical control across approximately 300 publicly verifiable infographics. Previously published on Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.18721380).

AI.tldr: SCHEMA is a structured prompt engineering framework for Google Gemini 3 Pro Image featuring a three-tier progressive system (BASE, MEDIO, AVANZATO) with 12 structured components, achieving 91-95% compliance rates across professional domains.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the lack of systematic, model-specific prompt engineering methodologies for AI image generation tools. While generic prompt guidelines exist, there is a need for a professional-grade, engineered framework that provides precise control over generation outcomes, particularly for high-stakes applications in real estate, commercial product photography, editorial content, storyboards, campaigns, and information design.
AI.method: Developed through systematic professional practice using 850 verified API predictions across 4,800 generated images spanning six domains. The methodology includes: (1) a three-tier progressive control system (BASE, MEDIO, AVANZATO) scaling from exploratory to directive control; (2) a modular label architecture with 7 core + 5 optional structured components; (3) a decision tree with explicit routing rules; (4) systematic documentation of model limitations with workarounds. Validation involved 621 structured prompts, batch consistency tests, 40 independent practitioners, and Information Design validation across 300 publicly verifiable infographics.
AI.result: Key findings include: 91% Mandatory compliance rate, 94% Prohibitions compliance rate across 621 structured prompts; substantially higher inter-generation coherence for structured prompts compared to unstructured ones; 95%+ first-generation compliance for spatial and typographical control in Information Design; independent practitioner validation confirming effectiveness across professional domains.
AI.conclusion: SCHEMA demonstrates that structured, tiered prompt engineering frameworks significantly improve control and consistency over AI image generation. The methodology provides a scalable approach that transitions practitioners from exploratory to directive control, with strong compliance rates (91-95%) and proven effectiveness across diverse professional applications. The framework's modular architecture allows for flexibility while maintaining rigorous control standards, making it suitable for high-stakes professional workflows.

title: SkillOrchestra: Learning to Route Agents via Skill Transfer
summary: Compound AI systems promise capabilities beyond those of individual models, yet their success depends critically on effective orchestration. Existing routing approaches face two limitations: (1) input-level routers make coarse query-level decisions that ignore evolving task requirements; (2) RL-trained orchestrators are expensive to adapt and often suffer from routing collapse, repeatedly invoking one strong but costly option in multi-turn scenarios. We introduce SkillOrchestra, a framework for skill-aware orchestration. Instead of directly learning a routing policy end-to-end, SkillOrchestra learns fine-grained skills from execution experience and models agent-specific competence and cost under those skills. At deployment, the orchestrator infers the skill demands of the current interaction and selects agents that best satisfy them under an explicit performance-cost trade-off. Extensive experiments across ten benchmarks demonstrate that SkillOrchestra outperforms SoTA RL-based orchestrators by up to 22.5% with 700x and 300x learning cost reduction compared to Router-R1 and ToolOrchestra, respectively. These results show that explicit skill modeling enables scalable, interpretable, and sample-efficient orchestration, offering a principled alternative to data-intensive RL-based approaches. The code is available at: https://github.com/jiayuww/SkillOrchestra.

AI.tldr: SkillOrchestra is a skill-aware orchestration framework that learns fine-grained skills from execution experience and models agent-specific competence and cost under those skills, outperforming existing RL-based orchestrators with significantly reduced learning costs.
AI.motivation: Existing routing approaches face two limitations: (1) input-level routers make coarse query-level decisions that ignore evolving task requirements; (2) RL-trained orchestrators are expensive to adapt and often suffer from routing collapse, repeatedly invoking one strong but costly option in multi-turn scenarios.
AI.method: SkillOrchestra learns fine-grained skills from execution experience and models agent-specific competence and cost under those skills. At deployment, the orchestrator infers the skill demands of the current interaction and selects agents that best satisfy them under an explicit performance-cost trade-off.
AI.result: SkillOrchestra outperforms state-of-the-art RL-based orchestrators by up to 22.5% with 700x and 300x learning cost reduction compared to Router-R1 and ToolOrchestra, respectively, across ten benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit skill modeling enables scalable, interpretable, and sample-efficient orchestration, offering a principled alternative to data-intensive RL-based approaches.

title: Toward Manifest Relationality in Transformers via Symmetry Reduction
summary: Transformer models contain substantial internal redundancy arising from coordinate-dependent representations and continuous symmetries, in model space and in head space, respectively. While recent approaches address this by explicitly breaking symmetry, we propose a complementary framework based on symmetry reduction. We reformulate representations, attention mechanisms, and optimization dynamics in terms of invariant relational quantities, eliminating redundant degrees of freedom by construction. This perspective yields architectures that operate directly on relational structures, providing a principled geometric framework for reducing parameter redundancy and analyzing optimization.

AI.tldr: Transformer models contain internal redundancy from coordinate-dependent representations and continuous symmetries. This paper proposes a symmetry reduction framework that reformulates representations, attention mechanisms, and optimization dynamics in terms of invariant relational quantities, eliminating redundant degrees of freedom by construction and yielding architectures operating directly on relational structures.
AI.motivation: Transformer models exhibit substantial internal redundancy arising from coordinate-dependent representations and continuous symmetries in both model space and head space.
AI.method: A complementary framework based on symmetry reduction that reformulates representations, attention mechanisms, and optimization dynamics in terms of invariant relational quantities, eliminating redundant degrees of freedom by construction.
AI.result: This perspective yields architectures that operate directly on relational structures, providing a principled geometric framework for reducing parameter redundancy and analyzing optimization dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The symmetry reduction framework offers a principled geometric approach to analyze and reduce parameter redundancy in transformer models.

title: EEG-Driven Intention Decoding: Offline Deep Learning Benchmarking on a Robotic Rover
summary: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) provide a hands-free control modality for mobile robotics, yet decoding user intent during real-world navigation remains challenging. This work presents a brain-robot control framework for offline decoding of driving commands during robotic rover operation. A 4WD Rover Pro platform was remotely operated by 12 participants who navigated a predefined route using a joystick, executing the commands forward, reverse, left, right, and stop. Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals were recorded with a 16-channel OpenBCI cap and aligned with motor actions at Delta = 0 ms and future prediction horizons (Delta > 0 ms). After preprocessing, several deep learning models were benchmarked, including convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, and Transformer architectures. ShallowConvNet achieved the highest performance for both action prediction and intent prediction. By combining real-world robotic control with multi-horizon EEG intention decoding, this study introduces a reproducible benchmark and reveals key design insights for predictive deep learning-based BCI systems.

AI.tldr: This study presents a brain-robot control framework for offline decoding of driving commands during robotic rover operation, benchmarking deep learning models (CNN, RNN, Transformer) on EEG signals to achieve high-performance action and intent prediction using a 4WD rover platform.
