title: One Size Fits None: Modeling NYC Taxi Trips
summary: The rise of app-based ride-sharing has fundamentally changed tipping culture in New York City. We analyzed 280 million trips from 2024 to see if we could predict tips for traditional taxis versus high-volume for-hire services. By testing methods from linear regression to deep neural networks, we found two very different outcomes. Traditional taxis are highly predictable ($R^2 \approx 0.72$) due to the in-car payment screen. In contrast, app-based tipping is random and hard to model ($R^2 \approx 0.17$). In conclusion, we show that building one universal model is a mistake and, due to Simpson's paradox, a combined model looks accurate on average but fails to predict tips for individual taxi categories requiring specialized models.

AI.tldr: The study finds that traditional NYC taxi tips are predictable ($R^2 \approx 0.72$) due to in-car payment screens, while app-based ride-sharing tips are random and hard to model ($R^2 \approx 0.17$). Combining these models leads to Simpson's paradox, where a single universal model appears accurate on average but fails for individual categories.
AI.motivation: The rise of app-based ride-sharing has fundamentally changed tipping culture in New York City, and the authors aim to investigate whether it's possible to predict tips for both traditional taxis and high-volume for-hire services.
AI.method: Analyzing 280 million trips from 2024 using various prediction methods ranging from linear regression to deep neural networks to compare tipping behaviors between traditional taxis and app-based services.
AI.result: Traditional taxis show highly predictable tipping patterns ($R^2 \approx 0.72$), while app-based tipping demonstrates randomness and low predictability ($R^2 \approx 0.17$).
AI.conclusion: Building a universal model for both service types is a mistake. Due to Simpson's paradox, a combined model may appear accurate on average but fails to predict tips for individual taxi categories, necessitating specialized models for each service type.

title: GS-CLIP: Zero-shot 3D Anomaly Detection by Geometry-Aware Prompt and Synergistic View Representation Learning
summary: Zero-shot 3D Anomaly Detection is an emerging task that aims to detect anomalies in a target dataset without any target training data, which is particularly important in scenarios constrained by sample scarcity and data privacy concerns. While current methods adapt CLIP by projecting 3D point clouds into 2D representations, they face challenges. The projection inherently loses some geometric details, and the reliance on a single 2D modality provides an incomplete visual understanding, limiting their ability to detect diverse anomaly types. To address these limitations, we propose the Geometry-Aware Prompt and Synergistic View Representation Learning (GS-CLIP) framework, which enables the model to identify geometric anomalies through a two-stage learning process. In stage 1, we dynamically generate text prompts embedded with 3D geometric priors. These prompts contain global shape context and local defect information distilled by our Geometric Defect Distillation Module (GDDM). In stage 2, we introduce Synergistic View Representation Learning architecture that processes rendered and depth images in parallel. A Synergistic Refinement Module (SRM) subsequently fuses the features of both streams, capitalizing on their complementary strengths. Comprehensive experimental results on four large-scale public datasets show that GS-CLIP achieves superior performance in detection. Code can be available at https://github.com/zhushengxinyue/GS-CLIP.

AI.tldr: GS-CLIP is a two-stage framework for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection that maintains geometric information through geometric defect distillation and synergistic view representation learning, outperforming current methods that project 3D point clouds to 2D representations.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection is critical for sample-scarce and privacy-constrained scenarios. Current methods project 3D point clouds to 2D representations using CLIP, but they lose geometric details and rely on a single 2D modality, limiting their ability to detect diverse anomaly types.
AI.method: GS-CLIP employs a two-stage learning process: (1) Geometric Defect Distillation Module (GDDM) generates text prompts embedded with 3D geometric priors containing global shape context and local defect information; (2) Synergistic View Representation Learning processes rendered and depth images in parallel, with a Synergistic Refinement Module (SRM) fusing complementary features from both streams.
AI.result: Comprehensive experimental results on four large-scale public datasets demonstrate that GS-CLIP achieves superior performance in anomaly detection compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: GS-CLIP effectively addresses the limitations of current zero-shot 3D anomaly detection approaches by maintaining geometric information through geometric priors and leveraging complementary views, enabling more robust detection of diverse anomaly types without requiring target training data.

title: LEVDA: Latent Ensemble Variational Data Assimilation via Differentiable Dynamics
summary: Long-range geophysical forecasts are fundamentally limited by chaotic dynamics and numerical errors. While data assimilation can mitigate these issues, classical variational smoothers require computationally expensive tangent-linear and adjoint models. Conversely, recent efficient latent filtering methods often enforce weak trajectory-level constraints and assume fixed observation grids. To bridge this gap, we propose Latent Ensemble Variational Data Assimilation (LEVDA), an ensemble-space variational smoother that operates in the low-dimensional latent space of a pretrained differentiable neural dynamics surrogate. By performing four-dimensional ensemble-variational (4DEnVar) optimization within an ensemble subspace, LEVDA jointly assimilates states and unknown parameters without the need for adjoint code or auxiliary observation-to-latent encoders. Leveraging the fully differentiable, continuous-in-time-and-space nature of the surrogate, LEVDA naturally accommodates highly irregular sampling at arbitrary spatiotemporal locations. Across three challenging geophysical benchmarks, LEVDA matches or outperforms state-of-the-art latent filtering baselines under severe observational sparsity while providing more reliable uncertainty quantification. Simultaneously, it achieves substantially improved assimilation accuracy and computational efficiency compared to full-state 4DEnVar.

AI.tldr: LEVDA is a novel data assimilation method that operates in the latent space of a neural surrogate to jointly assimilate states and parameters while accommodating irregular observations, outperforming both latent filtering and full-state variational approaches.
AI.motivation: Geophysical forecasts are limited by chaotic dynamics and numerical errors. Classical variational smoothers require computationally expensive tangent-linear and adjoint models, while recent latent filtering methods enforce weak trajectory-level constraints and assume fixed observation grids, creating a gap for efficient methods that can handle irregular sampling.
AI.method: LEVDA performs four-dimensional ensemble-variational optimization within an ensemble subspace of a pretrained differentiable neural dynamics surrogate. It jointly assimilates states and unknown parameters without adjoint code or auxiliary encoders, leveraging the surrogate's fully differentiable, continuous-in-time-and-space nature to accommodate highly irregular sampling at arbitrary spatiotemporal locations.
AI.result: Across three geophysical benchmarks, LEVDA matches or outperforms state-of-the-art latent filtering baselines under severe observational sparsity while providing more reliable uncertainty quantification, and achieves substantially improved assimilation accuracy and computational efficiency compared to full-state 4DEnVar.
AI.conclusion: LEVDA successfully bridges the gap between computationally expensive classical variational methods and weak-constraint latent filtering approaches, offering an efficient solution that handles irregular observations while maintaining accuracy and providing reliable uncertainty estimates.

title: SegMoTE: Token-Level Mixture of Experts for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation is vital for clinical diagnosis and quantitative analysis, yet remains challenging due to the heterogeneity of imaging modalities and the high cost of pixel-level annotations. Although general interactive segmentation models like SAM have achieved remarkable progress, their transfer to medical imaging still faces two key bottlenecks: (i) the lack of adaptive mechanisms for modality- and anatomy-specific tasks, which limits generalization in out-of-distribution medical scenarios; and (ii) current medical adaptation methods fine-tune on large, heterogeneous datasets without selection, leading to noisy supervision, higher cost, and negative transfer. To address these issues, we propose SegMoTE, an efficient and adaptive framework for medical image segmentation. SegMoTE preserves SAM's original prompt interface, efficient inference, and zero-shot generalization while introducing only a small number of learnable parameters to dynamically adapt across modalities and tasks. In addition, we design a progressive prompt tokenization mechanism that enables fully automatic segmentation, significantly reducing annotation dependence. Trained on MedSeg-HQ, a curated dataset less than 1% of existing large-scale datasets, SegMoTE achieves SOTA performance across diverse imaging modalities and anatomical tasks. It represents the first efficient, robust, and scalable adaptation of general segmentation models to the medical domain under extremely low annotation cost, advancing the practical deployment of foundation vision models in clinical applications.

AI.tldr: SegMoTE is an efficient and adaptive framework that adapts general segmentation models like SAM to medical imaging with minimal learnable parameters and automatic prompt tokenization, achieving SOTA performance across diverse medical modalities and anatomical tasks with extremely low annotation cost.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation faces two key bottlenecks when transferring general interactive segmentation models like SAM: (1) lack of adaptive mechanisms for modality- and anatomy-specific tasks limits generalization in out-of-distribution medical scenarios, and (2) current adaptation methods fine-tune on large, heterogeneous datasets without selection, leading to noisy supervision, higher cost, and negative transfer.
AI.method: SegMoTE preserves SAM's prompt interface, efficient inference, and zero-shot generalization while introducing a small number of learnable parameters to dynamically adapt across modalities and tasks. It incorporates a progressive prompt tokenization mechanism for fully automatic segmentation. The framework is trained on MedSeg-HQ, a curated dataset less than 1% of existing large-scale datasets.
AI.result: SegMoTE achieves SOTA performance across diverse imaging modalities and anatomical tasks, representing the first efficient, robust, and scalable adaptation of foundation vision models to the medical domain under extremely low annotation cost.
AI.conclusion: SegMoTE advances the practical deployment of foundation vision models in clinical applications by providing a scalable solution for medical image segmentation that overcomes the key bottlenecks of generalization and annotation cost.

title: Questions beyond Pixels: Integrating Commonsense Knowledge in Visual Question Generation for Remote Sensing
summary: With the rapid development of remote sensing image archives, asking questions about images has become an effective way of gathering specific information or performing semantic image retrieval. However, current automatically generated questions tend to be simplistic and template-based, which hinders the deployment of question answering or visual dialogue systems for real-world applications. To enrich and diversify the questions with both image content and commonsense knowledge, we propose a Knowledge-aware Remote Sensing Visual Question Generation model (KRSVQG). The proposed model incorporates related knowledge triplets from external knowledge sources to broaden the question content, while employing image captioning as an intermediary representation to ground questions to the corresponding images. Moreover, KRSVQG utilizes a vision-language pre-training and fine-tuning strategy, enabling the model's adaptation to low data regimes. To evaluate the proposed KRSVQG model, we construct two knowledge-aware remote sensing visual question generation datasets: the NWPU-300 dataset and the TextRS-300 dataset. Evaluations, including metrics and human assessment, demonstrate that KRSVQG outperforms existing methods and leads to rich questions, grounded in both image and domain knowledge. As a key practice in vision-language research, knowledge-aware visual question generation advances the understanding of image content beyond pixels, facilitating the development of knowledge-enriched vision-language systems with vision-grounded human commonsense.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Knowledge-aware Remote Sensing Visual Question Generation (KRSVQG) model that enriches and diversifies questions by incorporating external knowledge triplets and image captioning, outperforming existing methods on two new datasets.
AI.motivation: With the rapid development of remote sensing image archives, asking questions about images is an effective way of gathering specific information or performing semantic image retrieval. However, current automatically generated questions tend to be simplistic and template-based, which hinders the deployment of question answering or visual dialogue systems for real-world applications.
AI.method: The proposed KRSVQG model incorporates related knowledge triplets from external knowledge sources to broaden question content, employs image captioning as an intermediary representation to ground questions to corresponding images, and utilizes a vision-language pre-training and fine-tuning strategy for adaptation to low data regimes. Two datasets (NWPU-300 and TextRS-300) were constructed for evaluation.
AI.result: Evaluations including metrics and human assessment demonstrate that KRSVQG outperforms existing methods and leads to rich questions grounded in both image and domain knowledge.
AI.conclusion: As a key practice in vision-language research, knowledge-aware visual question generation advances the understanding of image content beyond pixels, facilitating the development of knowledge-enriched vision-language systems with vision-grounded human commonsense.

title: RAmmStein: Regime Adaptation in Mean-reverting Markets with Stein Thresholds -- Optimal Impulse Control in Concentrated AMMs
comment: 12 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables, 1 algorithm; submitted to Designing DeFi workshop (https://www.designingdefi.xyz/)
summary: Concentrated liquidity provision in decentralized exchanges presents a fundamental Impulse Control problem. Liquidity Providers (LPs) face a non-trivial trade-off between maximizing fee accrual through tight price-range concentration and minimizing the friction costs of rebalancing, including gas fees and swap slippage. Existing methods typically employ heuristic or threshold strategies that fail to account for market dynamics. This paper formulates liquidity management as an optimal control problem and derives the corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman quasi-variational inequality (HJB-QVI). We present an approximate solution RAmmStein, a Deep Reinforcement Learning method that incorporates the mean-reversion speed (theta) of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process among other features as input to the model. We demonstrate that the agent learns to separate the state space into regions of action and inaction. We evaluate the framework using high-frequency 1Hz Coinbase trade data comprising over 6.8M trades. Experimental results show that RAmmStein achieves a superior net ROI of 0.72% compared to both passive and aggressive strategies. Notably, the agent reduces rebalancing frequency by 67% compared to a greedy rebalancing strategy while maintaining 88% active time. Our results demonstrate that regime-aware laziness can significantly improve capital efficiency by preserving the returns that would otherwise be eroded by the operational costs.

AI.tldr: The paper formulates concentrated liquidity provision as an optimal control problem and introduces RAmmStein, a Deep Reinforcement Learning method that learns "regime-aware laziness" to optimize fee accrual while minimizing rebalancing costs, achieving 0.72% net ROI with 67% fewer rebalancings compared to baseline strategies.
AI.motivation: Liquidity Providers (LPs) face a trade-off between maximizing fee accrual through tight price-range concentration and minimizing friction costs (gas fees and swap slippage). Existing heuristic or threshold strategies fail to account for market dynamics.
AI.method: Formulates liquidity management as an optimal control problem and derives the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman quasi-variational inequality (HJB-QVI). Presents RAmmStein, a Deep Reinforcement Learning method incorporating the mean-reversion speed (theta) of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process as input features.
AI.result: On high-frequency Coinbase data (6.8M trades), RAmmStein achieves superior net ROI of 0.72% compared to passive and aggressive strategies. Reduces rebalancing frequency by 67% while maintaining 88% active time. The agent learns to separate state space into regions of action and inaction.
AI.conclusion: Regime-aware laziness can significantly improve capital efficiency by preserving returns that would otherwise be eroded by operational costs.

title: Controlled Face Manipulation and Synthesis for Data Augmentation
summary: Deep learning vision models excel with abundant supervision, but many applications face label scarcity and class imbalance. Controllable image editing can augment scarce labeled data, yet edits often introduce artifacts and entangle non-target attributes. We study this in facial expression analysis, targeting Action Unit (AU) manipulation where annotation is costly and AU co-activation drives entanglement. We present a facial manipulation method that operates in the semantic latent space of a pre-trained face generator (Diffusion Autoencoder). Using lightweight linear models, we reduce entanglement of semantic features via (i) dependency-aware conditioning that accounts for AU co-activation, and (ii) orthogonal projection that removes nuisance attribute directions (e.g., glasses), together with an expression neutralization step to enable absolute AU edit. We use these edits to balance AU occurrence by editing labeled faces and to diversify identities/demographics via controlled synthesis. Augmenting AU detector training with the generated data improves accuracy and yields more disentangled predictions with fewer co-activation shortcuts, outperforming alternative data-efficient training strategies and suggesting improvements similar to what would require substantially more labeled data in our learning-curve analysis. Compared to prior methods, our edits are stronger, produce fewer artifacts, and preserve identity better.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a facial expression editing method operating in the semantic latent space of a Diffusion Autoencoder using lightweight linear models to reduce attribute entanglement via dependency-aware conditioning, orthogonal projection, and expression neutralization. These edits are used to augment AU detector training data, significantly improving accuracy and prediction disentanglement while outperforming alternative data-efficient strategies.
AI.motivation: Deep learning vision models require abundant supervision, yet many real-world applications face label scarcity and class imbalance. Controllable image editing can augment scarce labeled data, but existing methods often introduce artifacts and entangle non-target attributes, particularly in facial expression analysis where Action Unit (AU) manipulation suffers from costly annotation and AU co-activation-driven entanglement.
AI.method: The method operates in the semantic latent space of a pre-trained face Diffusion Autoencoder. It employs lightweight linear models to implement three key components: (1) dependency-aware conditioning that accounts for AU co-activation patterns, (2) orthogonal projection to remove nuisance attribute directions (e.g., glasses), and (3) expression neutralization to enable absolute AU editing. The approach enables both balanced AU occurrence augmentation and controlled synthesis for identity/diversity augmentation.
AI.result: Augmenting AU detector training with generated data improves accuracy and yields more disentangled predictions with fewer co-activation shortcuts. The approach outperforms alternative data-efficient training strategies and achieves improvements comparable to what would require substantially more labeled data in learning-curve analysis. Compared to prior methods, the edits are stronger, produce fewer artifacts, and better preserve identity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed latent space manipulation framework effectively addresses AU entanglement in facial expression editing while maintaining identity preservation and edit fidelity. The data augmentation approach demonstrates significant performance gains with minimal labeled data requirements, suggesting a practical solution for label-scarce facial expression analysis tasks.

title: PIS: A Physics-Informed System for Accurate State Partitioning of $Aβ_{42}$ Protein Trajectories
summary: Understanding the conformational evolution of $β$-amyloid ($Aβ$), particularly the $Aβ_{42}$ isoform, is fundamental to elucidating the pathogenic mechanisms underlying Alzheimer's disease. However, existing end-to-end deep learning models often struggle to capture subtle state transitions in protein trajectories due to a lack of explicit physical constraints. In this work, we introduce PIS, a Physics-Informed System designed for robust metastable state partitioning. By integrating pre-computed physical priors, such as the radius of gyration and solvent-accessible surface area, into the extraction of topological features, our model achieves superior performance on the $Aβ_{42}$ dataset. Furthermore, PIS provides an interactive platform that features dynamic monitoring of physical characteristics and multi-dimensional result validation. This system offers biological researchers a powerful set of analytical tools with physically grounded interpretability. A demonstration video of PIS is available on https://youtu.be/AJHGzUtRCg0.

AI.tldr: PIS is a Physics-Informed System for robust metastable state partitioning of Aβ42 protein trajectories, integrating physical priors (radius of gyration and solvent-accessible surface area) to capture subtle state transitions that existing deep learning models miss.
AI.motivation: Understanding the conformational evolution of β-amyloid (Aβ), particularly the Aβ42 isoform, is fundamental to elucidating Alzheimer's disease pathogenic mechanisms. However, existing end-to-end deep learning models struggle to capture subtle state transitions in protein trajectories due to a lack of explicit physical constraints.
AI.method: PIS (Physics-Informed System) integrates pre-computed physical priors—such as radius of gyration and solvent-accessible surface area—into the extraction of topological features for metastable state partitioning. The system provides an interactive platform with dynamic monitoring of physical characteristics and multi-dimensional result validation.
AI.result: PIS achieves superior performance on the Aβ42 dataset compared to existing approaches. The system provides biological researchers with powerful analytical tools that offer physically grounded interpretability.
AI.conclusion: By combining physical priors with deep learning, PIS enables more accurate capture of subtle protein state transitions, providing biologically meaningful insights into Aβ42 conformational evolution and offering a valuable tool for Alzheimer's disease research.

title: Knowledge-aware Visual Question Generation for Remote Sensing Images
summary: With the rapid development of remote sensing image archives, asking questions about images has become an effective way of gathering specific information or performing image retrieval. However, automatically generated image-based questions tend to be simplistic and template-based, which hinders the real deployment of question answering or visual dialogue systems. To enrich and diversify the questions, we propose a knowledge-aware remote sensing visual question generation model, KRSVQG, that incorporates external knowledge related to the image content to improve the quality and contextual understanding of the generated questions. The model takes an image and a related knowledge triplet from external knowledge sources as inputs and leverages image captioning as an intermediary representation to enhance the image grounding of the generated questions. To assess the performance of KRSVQG, we utilized two datasets that we manually annotated: NWPU-300 and TextRS-300. Results on these two datasets demonstrate that KRSVQG outperforms existing methods and leads to knowledge-enriched questions, grounded in both image and domain knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes KRSVQG, a knowledge-aware remote sensing visual question generation model that incorporates external knowledge triplets and uses image captioning as an intermediary representation to generate more diverse and contextually grounded questions for remote sensing images.
AI.motivation: With the rapid growth of remote sensing image archives, question answering systems need high-quality questions. However, automatically generated image-based questions tend to be simplistic and template-based, hindering real-world deployment of visual dialogue systems.
AI.method: KRSVQG takes an image and a related knowledge triplet from external sources as inputs. It uses image captioning as an intermediary representation to enhance image grounding, incorporating external knowledge to improve the contextual understanding and quality of generated questions.
AI.result: Evaluated on two manually annotated datasets (NWPU-300 and TextRS-300), KRSVQG outperforms existing methods and generates knowledge-enriched questions that are grounded in both image content and domain knowledge.
AI.conclusion: KRSVQG effectively addresses the limitation of simplistic template-based questions by incorporating external knowledge and image grounding techniques, leading to more diverse and contextually appropriate questions for remote sensing image QA systems.

title: No Need For Real Anomaly: MLLM Empowered Zero-Shot Video Anomaly Detection
summary: The collection and detection of video anomaly data has long been a challenging problem due to its rare occurrence and spatio-temporal scarcity. Existing video anomaly detection (VAD) methods under perform in open-world scenarios. Key contributing factors include limited dataset diversity, and inadequate understanding of context-dependent anomalous semantics. To address these issues, i) we propose LAVIDA, an end-to-end zero-shot video anomaly detection framework. ii) LAVIDA employs an Anomaly Exposure Sampler that transforms segmented objects into pseudo-anomalies to enhance model adaptability to unseen anomaly categories. It further integrates a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) to bolster semantic comprehension capabilities. Additionally, iii) we design a token compression approach based on reverse attention to handle the spatio-temporal scarcity of anomalous patterns and decrease computational cost. The training process is conducted solely on pseudo anomalies without any VAD data. Evaluations across four benchmark VAD datasets demonstrate that LAVIDA achieves SOTA performance in both frame-level and pixel-level anomaly detection under the zero-shot setting. Our code is available in https://github.com/VitaminCreed/LAVIDA.

AI.tldr: LAVIDA is an end-to-end zero-shot video anomaly detection framework that uses pseudo-anomaly exposure sampling, multimodal large language models, and token compression to achieve state-of-the-art performance on four benchmark datasets without requiring any real VAD data during training.
AI.motivation: Video anomaly detection is challenging due to rare occurrence, spatio-temporal scarcity, and poor performance in open-world scenarios caused by limited dataset diversity and inadequate understanding of context-dependent anomalous semantics.
AI.method: LAVIDA employs three key components: (1) Anomaly Exposure Sampler that transforms segmented objects into pseudo-anomalies to enhance model adaptability; (2) Integration of a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for improved semantic comprehension; (3) Token compression approach based on reverse attention to handle spatio-temporal scarcity and reduce computational cost. Training is conducted solely on pseudo anomalies without any real VAD data.
AI.result: Evaluations across four benchmark VAD datasets demonstrate that LAVIDA achieves state-of-the-art performance in both frame-level and pixel-level anomaly detection under the zero-shot setting.
AI.conclusion: LAVIDA successfully addresses the challenges of rare occurrence and spatio-temporal scarcity in video anomaly detection, achieving superior performance without requiring real VAD data during training through its innovative combination of pseudo-anomaly exposure, multimodal semantic understanding, and efficient token compression.

title: Making Conformal Predictors Robust in Healthcare Settings: a Case Study on EEG Classification
summary: Quantifying uncertainty in clinical predictions is critical for high-stakes diagnosis tasks. Conformal prediction offers a principled approach by providing prediction sets with theoretical coverage guarantees. However, in practice, patient distribution shifts violate the i.i.d. assumptions underlying standard conformal methods, leading to poor coverage in healthcare settings. In this work, we evaluate several conformal prediction approaches on EEG seizure classification, a task with known distribution shift challenges and label uncertainty. We demonstrate that personalized calibration strategies can improve coverage by over 20 percentage points while maintaining comparable prediction set sizes. Our implementation is available via PyHealth, an open-source healthcare AI framework: https://github.com/sunlabuiuc/PyHealth.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates conformal prediction methods for EEG seizure classification under distribution shift conditions, demonstrating that personalized calibration strategies can improve coverage by over 20 percentage points while maintaining comparable prediction set sizes.
AI.motivation: Quantifying uncertainty in clinical predictions is critical for high-stakes diagnosis tasks. Conformal prediction offers a principled approach with theoretical coverage guarantees, but patient distribution shifts violate the i.i.d. assumptions underlying standard conformal methods, leading to poor coverage in healthcare settings. EEG seizure classification presents additional challenges including known distribution shifts and label uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors evaluate several conformal prediction approaches on EEG seizure classification, a task characterized by distribution shift challenges and label uncertainty. They test both standard conformal methods and personalized calibration strategies to compare their performance in addressing these issues. Their implementation is available through PyHealth, an open-source healthcare AI framework.
AI.result: Personalized calibration strategies achieve coverage improvements of over 20 percentage points compared to standard conformal methods, while maintaining comparable prediction set sizes. This demonstrates that personalized approaches can effectively address the coverage deficits caused by distribution shifts in healthcare applications.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that personalized calibration strategies are effective for improving coverage in healthcare applications subject to distribution shifts, offering a practical solution for high-stakes clinical prediction tasks where uncertainty quantification is essential. The availability of an open-source implementation through PyHealth facilitates adoption and further research.

title: RegionRoute: Regional Style Transfer with Diffusion Model
summary: Precise spatial control in diffusion-based style transfer remains challenging. This challenge arises because diffusion models treat style as a global feature and lack explicit spatial grounding of style representations, making it difficult to restrict style application to specific objects or regions. To our knowledge, existing diffusion models are unable to perform true localized style transfer, typically relying on handcrafted masks or multi-stage post-processing that introduce boundary artifacts and limit generalization. To address this, we propose an attention-supervised diffusion framework that explicitly teaches the model where to apply a given style by aligning the attention scores of style tokens with object masks during training. Two complementary objectives, a Focus loss based on KL divergence and a Cover loss using binary cross-entropy, jointly encourage accurate localization and dense coverage. A modular LoRA-MoE design further enables efficient and scalable multi-style adaptation. To evaluate localized stylization, we introduce the Regional Style Editing Score, which measures Regional Style Matching through CLIP-based similarity within the target region and Identity Preservation via masked LPIPS and pixel-level consistency on unedited areas. Experiments show that our method achieves mask-free, single-object style transfer at inference, producing regionally accurate and visually coherent results that outperform existing diffusion-based editing approaches.

AI.tldr: An attention-supervised diffusion framework that enables mask-free, single-object style transfer by aligning style token attention with object masks, using Focus and Cover losses for localization and coverage, along with a modular LoRA-MoE design for multi-style adaptation.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models treat style as global features without explicit spatial grounding, making it difficult to apply style to specific objects or regions. Existing methods rely on handcrafted masks or multi-stage post-processing that introduce boundary artifacts and limit generalization, preventing true localized style transfer.
AI.method: Proposed attention-supervised diffusion framework that aligns style token attention scores with object masks during training. Two complementary objectives are used: Focus loss based on KL divergence and Cover loss using binary cross-entropy. A modular LoRA-MoE design enables efficient multi-style adaptation.
AI.result: Achieves mask-free, single-object style transfer at inference, producing regionally accurate and visually coherent results that outperform existing diffusion-based editing approaches. The method enables true localized style transfer without boundary artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed attention-supervised diffusion framework successfully addresses the challenge of precise spatial control in diffusion-based style transfer by teaching the model where to apply style through attention supervision. The combination of Focus and Cover losses enables accurate localization and dense coverage, while the LoRA-MoE design supports efficient multi-style adaptation, demonstrating that true localized style transfer is achievable in diffusion models.

title: Federated Learning Playground
summary: We present Federated Learning Playground, an interactive browser-based platform inspired by and extends TensorFlow Playground that teaches core Federated Learning (FL) concepts. Users can experiment with heterogeneous client data distributions, model hyperparameters, and aggregation algorithms directly in the browser without coding or system setup, and observe their effects on client and global models through real-time visualizations, gaining intuition for challenges such as non-IID data, local overfitting, and scalability. The playground serves as an easy to use educational tool, lowering the entry barrier for newcomers to distributed AI while also offering a sandbox for rapidly prototyping and comparing FL methods. By democratizing exploration of FL, it promotes broader understanding and adoption of this important paradigm.

AI.tldr: Federated Learning Playground is a browser-based interactive platform inspired by TensorFlow Playground that enables users to experiment with federated learning concepts, heterogeneous client data, hyperparameters, and aggregation algorithms without coding, visualizing real-time model behavior and gaining intuition for FL challenges.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for accessible educational tools for federated learning (FL) to lower entry barriers for newcomers and provide a sandbox for rapid prototyping and method comparison. It aims to democratize exploration of FL and promote broader understanding and adoption of this important distributed AI paradigm.
AI.method: The platform is implemented as a browser-based application extending TensorFlow Playground's interactive approach. It allows users to experiment with heterogeneous client data distributions, model hyperparameters, and aggregation algorithms without requiring coding or system setup. Real-time visualizations track client and global model performance during training.
AI.result: Users can directly observe how different configurations affect client and global models through interactive visualizations. The platform enables gaining intuition for key FL challenges such as non-IID data, local overfitting, and scalability. It functions both as an educational tool and a prototyping sandbox for FL methods.
AI.conclusion: By democratizing FL exploration through an accessible browser-based interface, the platform promotes broader understanding and adoption of federated learning, making it easier for newcomers to learn core concepts and for researchers to quickly prototype and compare different FL approaches.

title: DD-CAM: Minimal Sufficient Explanations for Vision Models Using Delta Debugging
summary: We introduce a gradient-free framework for identifying minimal, sufficient, and decision-preserving explanations in vision models by isolating the smallest subset of representational units whose joint activation preserves predictions. Unlike existing approaches that aggregate all units, often leading to cluttered saliency maps, our approach, DD-CAM, identifies a 1-minimal subset whose joint activation suffices to preserve the prediction (i.e., removing any unit from the subset alters the prediction). To efficiently isolate minimal sufficient subsets, we adapt delta debugging, a systematic reduction strategy from software debugging, and configure its search strategy based on unit interactions in the classifier head: testing individual units for models with non-interacting units and testing unit combinations for models in which unit interactions exist. We then generate minimal, prediction-preserving saliency maps that highlight only the most essential features. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that our approach can produce more faithful explanations and achieve higher localization accuracy than the state-of-the-art CAM-based approaches.

AI.tldr: DD-CAM is a gradient-free framework that identifies minimal, sufficient, and decision-preserving explanations in vision models by isolating the smallest subset of representational units whose joint activation preserves predictions. It adapts delta debugging to efficiently find minimal subsets and generates more faithful explanations than state-of-the-art CAM-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches aggregate all units in explanation generation, leading to cluttered saliency maps that lack interpretability. The authors aim to find minimal, sufficient subsets whose joint activation preserves predictions to produce more faithful and interpretable explanations.
AI.method: DD-CAM adapts delta debugging, a systematic reduction strategy from software debugging, to efficiently isolate minimal sufficient subsets. The search strategy is configured based on unit interactions: testing individual units for models with non-interacting units, and testing unit combinations for models with interactions. It identifies 1-minimal subsets where removing any unit alters the prediction.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation demonstrates that DD-CAM produces more faithful explanations and achieves higher localization accuracy than state-of-the-art CAM-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: DD-CAM successfully identifies minimal sufficient subsets and generates more interpretable and accurate explanations compared to existing methods that aggregate all units.

title: Softmax is not Enough (for Adaptive Conformal Classification)
summary: The merit of Conformal Prediction (CP), as a distribution-free framework for uncertainty quantification, depends on generating prediction sets that are efficient, reflected in small average set sizes, while adaptive, meaning they signal uncertainty by varying in size according to input difficulty. A central limitation for deep conformal classifiers is that the nonconformity scores are derived from softmax outputs, which can be unreliable indicators of how certain the model truly is about a given input, sometimes leading to overconfident misclassifications or undue hesitation. In this work, we argue that this unreliability can be inherited by the prediction sets generated by CP, limiting their capacity for adaptiveness. We propose a new approach that leverages information from the pre-softmax logit space, using the Helmholtz Free Energy as a measure of model uncertainty and sample difficulty. By reweighting nonconformity scores with a monotonic transformation of the energy score of each sample, we improve their sensitivity to input difficulty. Our experiments with four state-of-the-art score functions on multiple datasets and deep architectures show that this energy-based enhancement improves the adaptiveness of the prediction sets, leading to a notable increase in both efficiency and adaptiveness compared to baseline nonconformity scores, without introducing any post-hoc complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses a key limitation in deep conformal classifiers where softmax-based nonconformity scores are unreliable indicators of model uncertainty. The authors propose using Helmholtz Free Energy from the pre-softmax logit space to improve uncertainty quantification, demonstrating enhanced adaptiveness and efficiency in prediction sets across multiple datasets and architectures.
AI.motivation: Conformal Prediction (CP) effectiveness depends on generating efficient (small average set sizes) and adaptive prediction sets that vary based on input difficulty. A central limitation in deep conformal classifiers is that nonconformity scores derived from softmax outputs are unreliable indicators of model certainty, often leading to overconfident misclassifications or undue hesitation, which undermines the adaptiveness of generated prediction sets.
AI.method: The authors propose using information from pre-softmax logit space, specifically the Helmholtz Free Energy as a measure of model uncertainty and sample difficulty. They reweight nonconformity scores using a monotonic transformation of the energy score of each sample, leveraging energy-based measures to better capture uncertainty and input difficulty.
AI.result: Experiments with four state-of-the-art score functions on multiple datasets and deep architectures demonstrate that the energy-based enhancement improves the adaptiveness of prediction sets, resulting in a notable increase in both efficiency and adaptiveness compared to baseline nonconformity scores. The improvements are achieved without introducing any additional post-hoc complexity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the unreliability of softmax-based nonconformity scores in deep conformal classifiers by incorporating Helmholtz Free Energy from the pre-softmax logit space. This energy-based enhancement significantly improves the adaptiveness and efficiency of prediction sets while maintaining simplicity by avoiding additional post-hoc complexity.

title: A Two-Stage Detection-Tracking Framework for Stable Apple Quality Inspection in Dense Conveyor-Belt Environments
summary: Industrial fruit inspection systems must operate reliably under dense multi-object interactions and continuous motion, yet most existing works evaluate detection or classification at the image level without ensuring temporal stability in video streams. We present a two-stage detection-tracking framework for stable multi-apple quality inspection in conveyor-belt environments. An orchard-trained YOLOv8 model performs apple localization, followed by ByteTrack multi-object tracking to maintain persistent identities. A ResNet18 defect classifier, fine-tuned on a healthy-defective fruit dataset, is applied to cropped apple regions. Track-level aggregation is introduced to enforce temporal consistency and reduce prediction oscillation across frames. We define video-level industrial metrics such as track-level defect ratio and temporal consistency to evaluate system robustness under realistic processing conditions. Results demonstrate improved stability compared to frame-wise inference, suggesting that integrating tracking is essential for practical automated fruit grading systems.

AI.tldr: A two-stage detection-tracking framework using YOLOv8 for apple localization, ByteTrack for multi-object tracking, and a fine-tuned ResNet18 classifier with track-level aggregation to achieve stable multi-apple quality inspection in conveyor-belt environments under dense multi-object interactions and continuous motion.
AI.motivation: Industrial fruit inspection systems must operate reliably under dense multi-object interactions and continuous motion, yet most existing works evaluate detection or classification at the image level without ensuring temporal stability in video streams, making them impractical for real-world conveyor-belt environments.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) YOLOv8 orchard-trained model for apple localization, (2) ByteTrack multi-object tracking to maintain persistent identities, followed by ResNet18 defect classifier fine-tuned on healthy-defective fruit dataset applied to cropped apple regions, with track-level aggregation to enforce temporal consistency and reduce prediction oscillation.
AI.result: Results demonstrate improved stability compared to frame-wise inference, with video-level industrial metrics (track-level defect ratio and temporal consistency) showing the system's robustness under realistic processing conditions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating tracking is essential for practical automated fruit grading systems, as demonstrated by the improved temporal stability and robustness achieved through the proposed framework.

title: Less is More: Convergence Benefits of Fewer Data Weight Updates over Longer Horizon
summary: Data mixing--the strategic reweighting of training domains--is a critical component in training robust machine learning models. This problem is naturally formulated as a bilevel optimization task, where the outer loop optimizes domain weights to minimize validation loss, and the inner loop optimizes model parameters to minimize the weighted training loss. Classical bilevel optimization relies on hypergradients, which theoretically require the inner optimization to reach convergence. However, due to computational constraints, state-of-the-art methods use a finite, often small, number of inner update steps before updating the weights. The theoretical implications of this approximation are not well understood. In this work, we rigorously analyze the convergence behavior of data mixing with a finite number of inner steps $T$. We prove that the "greedy" practical approach of using $T=1$ can fail even in a simple quadratic example. Under a fixed parameter update budget $N$ and assuming the per-domain losses are strongly convex, we show that the optimal $T$ scales as $Θ(\log N)$ (resp., $Θ({(N \log N)}^{1/2})$) for the data mixing problem with access to full (resp., stochastic) gradients. We complement our theoretical results with proof-of-concept experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes data mixing in bilevel optimization for robust ML models, investigating convergence behavior with a finite number of inner update steps T. It shows T=1 can fail even in simple cases, and derives optimal T scaling as Θ(log N) for full gradients or Θ((N log N)^1/2) for stochastic gradients under a fixed parameter update budget N.
AI.motivation: Data mixing is critical for training robust ML models but is formulated as bilevel optimization where classical methods rely on inner convergence via hypergradients. Practical methods use finite inner steps due to computational constraints, yet the theoretical implications of this approximation were not well understood.
AI.method: The authors rigorously analyze convergence behavior with finite T, proving T=1 can fail in simple quadratic examples. Under a fixed parameter update budget N and assuming strongly convex per-domain losses, they derive optimal T scaling: Θ(log N) for full gradient access and Θ((N log N)^1/2) for stochastic gradients.
AI.result: They show that the greedy approach of using T=1 can fail even in simple cases. The optimal number of inner steps scales as Θ(log N) (full gradients) or Θ((N log N)^1/2) (stochastic gradients) under the given constraints.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical results provide important guidance for choosing appropriate inner step counts in data mixing, complemented by proof-of-concept experiments, bridging the gap between classical theory and practical implementation.

title: MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World Model
summary: Clinical MRI contrast acquisition suffers from inefficient information yield, which presents as a mismatch between the risky and costly acquisition protocol and the fixed and sparse acquisition sequence. Applying world models to simulate the contrast enhancement kinetics in the human body enables continuous contrast-free dynamics. However, the low temporal resolution in MRI acquisition restricts the training of world models, leading to a sparsely sampled dataset. Directly training a generative model to capture the kinetics leads to two limitations: (a) Due to the absence of data on missing time, the model tends to overfit to irrelevant features, leading to content distortion. (b) Due to the lack of continuous temporal supervision, the model fails to learn the continuous kinetics law over time, causing temporal discontinuities. For the first time, we propose MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World model (MRI CEKWorld) with SpatioTemporal Consistency Learning (STCL). For (a), guided by the spatial law that patient-level structures remain consistent during enhancement, we propose Latent Alignment Learning (LAL) that constructs a patient-specific template to constrain contents to align with this template. For (b), guided by the temporal law that the kinetics follow a consistent smooth trend, we propose Latent Difference Learning (LDL) which extends the unobserved intervals by interpolation and constrains smooth variations in the latent space among interpolated sequences. Extensive experiments on two datasets show our MRI CEKWorld achieves better realistic contents and kinetics. Codes will be available at https://github.com/DD0922/MRI-Contrast-Enhancement-Kinetics-World-Model.

AI.tldr: A novel MRI CEKWorld model with SpatioTemporal Consistency Learning (STCL) that addresses overfitting and temporal discontinuities in contrast enhancement kinetics prediction by introducing Latent Alignment Learning (LAL) and Latent Difference Learning (LDL).
AI.motivation: Clinical MRI contrast acquisition suffers from inefficient information yield and mismatch between acquisition protocol and fixed sparse sequence. While world models can simulate contrast enhancement kinetics, low temporal resolution in MRI limits training data, causing overfitting and temporal discontinuities in generative models.
AI.method: MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World model (MRI CEKWorld) with SpatioTemporal Consistency Learning (STCL), featuring two novel components: (1) Latent Alignment Learning (LAL) using spatial law to constrain content alignment with patient-specific templates; (2) Latent Difference Learning (LDL) using temporal law to extend unobserved intervals via interpolation and constrain smooth latent variations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two datasets demonstrate that MRI CEKWorld achieves better realistic contents and kinetics compared to direct generative model training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed STCL framework effectively addresses the two main limitations of directly training generative models for MRI contrast enhancement kinetics—overfitting to irrelevant features and temporal discontinuities—by leveraging both spatial consistency (patient structures) and temporal smoothness (kinetics trends) laws.

title: Variational Trajectory Optimization of Anisotropic Diffusion Schedules
summary: We introduce a variational framework for diffusion models with anisotropic noise schedules parameterized by a matrix-valued path $M_t(θ)$ that allocates noise across subspaces. Central to our framework is a trajectory-level objective that jointly trains the score network and learns $M_t(θ)$, which encompasses general parameterization classes of matrix-valued noise schedules. We further derive an estimator for the derivative with respect to $θ$ of the score that enables efficient optimization of the $M_t(θ)$ schedule. For inference, we develop an efficiently-implementable reverse-ODE solver that is an anisotropic generalization of the second-order Heun discretization algorithm. Across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64, our method consistently improves upon the baseline EDM model in all NFE regimes. Code is available at https://github.com/lizeyu090312/anisotropic-diffusion-paper.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a variational framework for diffusion models with anisotropic noise schedules parameterized by a matrix-valued path M_t(θ), jointly training the score network and the schedule. It develops an efficient reverse-ODE solver for inference and consistently improves upon the baseline EDM model across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64 in all NFE regimes.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve diffusion models by introducing anisotropic noise schedules that allocate noise across subspaces, moving beyond isotropic Gaussian noise assumptions to better capture complex data distributions.
AI.method: A variational framework with matrix-valued noise schedule M_t(θ) parameterized by θ. Key components include: (1) a trajectory-level objective that jointly trains the score network and M_t(θ); (2) an estimator for the derivative of the score with respect to θ for efficient optimization; (3) a reverse-ODE solver that is an anisotropic generalization of the second-order Heun discretization algorithm.
AI.result: The method consistently improves upon the baseline EDM model across all NFE regimes on CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64 datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework with anisotropic noise schedules and efficient inference solver is effective and generalizable across different datasets and evaluation regimes. The implementation is publicly available.

title: IPv2: An Improved Image Purification Strategy for Real-World Ultra-Low-Dose Lung CT Denoising
summary: The image purification strategy constructs an intermediate distribution with aligned anatomical structures, which effectively corrects the spatial misalignment between real-world ultra-low-dose CT and normal-dose CT images and significantly enhances the structural preservation ability of denoising models. However, this strategy exhibits two inherent limitations. First, it suppresses noise only in the chest wall and bone regions while leaving the image background untreated. Second, it lacks a dedicated mechanism for denoising the lung parenchyma. To address these issues, we systematically redesign the original image purification strategy and propose an improved version termed IPv2. The proposed strategy introduces three core modules, namely Remove Background, Add noise, and Remove noise. These modules endow the model with denoising capability in both background and lung tissue regions during training data construction and provide a more reasonable evaluation protocol through refined label construction at the testing stage. Extensive experiments on our previously established real-world patient lung CT dataset acquired at 2% radiation dose demonstrate that IPv2 consistently improves background suppression and lung parenchyma restoration across multiple mainstream denoising models. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/MonkeyDadLufy/Image-Purification-Strategy-v2.

AI.tldr: IPv2 is an improved image purification strategy that addresses limitations of the original approach by introducing three core modules (Remove Background, Add noise, Remove noise) to enable denoising in both background and lung tissue regions, demonstrating consistent improvements in background suppression and lung parenchyma restoration across multiple denoising models for ultra-low-dose CT images.
AI.motivation: The original image purification strategy effectively corrects spatial misalignment between real-world ultra-low-dose CT and normal-dose CT images but suffers from two inherent limitations: (1) it suppresses noise only in chest wall and bone regions while leaving the image background untreated, and (2) it lacks a dedicated mechanism for denoising the lung parenchyma.
AI.method: IPv2 systematically redesigns the original strategy by introducing three core modules during training data construction: Remove Background (to handle background regions), Add noise (to simulate noise), and Remove noise (to enable denoising capability). Additionally, it provides a more reasonable evaluation protocol through refined label construction at the testing stage.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a real-world patient lung CT dataset acquired at 2% radiation dose demonstrate that IPv2 consistently improves background suppression and lung parenchyma restoration across multiple mainstream denoising models.
AI.conclusion: IPv2 successfully addresses the limitations of the original image purification strategy by enabling effective denoising in both background and lung tissue regions, establishing a more reasonable evaluation protocol, and providing a publicly available implementation (https://github.com/MonkeyDadLufy/Image-Purification-Strategy-v2) that enhances structural preservation ability of denoising models for ultra-low-dose CT images.

title: Beyond Accuracy: A Unified Random Matrix Theory Diagnostic Framework for Crash Classification Models
summary: Crash classification models in transportation safety are typically evaluated using accuracy, F1, or AUC, metrics that cannot reveal whether a model is silently overfitting. We introduce a spectral diagnostic framework grounded in Random Matrix Theory (RMT) and Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HTSR) that spans the ML taxonomy: weight matrices for BERT/ALBERT/Qwen2.5, out-of-fold increment matrices for XGBoost/Random Forest, empirical Hessians for Logistic Regression, induced affinity matrices for Decision Trees, and Graph Laplacians for KNN. Evaluating nine model families on two Iowa DOT crash classification tasks (173,512 and 371,062 records respectively), we find that the power-law exponent $α$ provides a structural quality signal: well-regularized models consistently yield $α$ within $[2, 4]$ (mean $2.87 \pm 0.34$), while overfit variants show $α< 2$ or spectral collapse. We observe a strong rank correlation between $α$ and expert agreement (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$, $p < 0.001$), suggesting spectral quality captures model behaviors aligned with expert reasoning. We propose an $α$-based early stopping criterion and a spectral model selection protocol, and validate both against cross-validated F1 baselines. Sparse Lanczos approximations make the framework scalable to large datasets.

AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

title: Pay Attention to CTC: Fast and Robust Pseudo-Labelling for Unified Speech Recognition
summary: Unified Speech Recognition (USR) has emerged as a semi-supervised framework for training a single model for audio, visual, and audiovisual speech recognition, achieving state-of-the-art results on in-distribution benchmarks. However, its reliance on autoregressive pseudo-labelling makes training expensive, while its decoupled supervision of CTC and attention branches increases susceptibility to self-reinforcing errors, particularly under distribution shifts involving longer sequences, noise, or unseen domains. We propose CTC-driven teacher forcing, where greedily decoded CTC pseudo-labels are fed into the decoder to generate attention targets in a single forward pass. Although these can be globally incoherent, in the pseudo-labelling setting they enable efficient and effective knowledge transfer. Because CTC and CTC-driven attention pseudo-labels have the same length, the decoder can predict both simultaneously, benefiting from the robustness of CTC and the expressiveness of attention without costly beam search. We further propose mixed sampling to mitigate the exposure bias of the decoder relying solely on CTC inputs. The resulting method, USR 2.0, halves training time, improves robustness to out-of-distribution inputs, and achieves state-of-the-art results on LRS3, LRS2, and WildVSR, surpassing USR and modality-specific self-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: USR 2.0 is an efficient unified speech recognition framework that uses CTC-driven teacher forcing and mixed sampling to reduce training time and improve robustness over distribution shifts, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The original USR framework relies on expensive autoregressive pseudo-labeling and suffers from decoupled supervision that increases susceptibility to self-reinforcing errors, particularly under distribution shifts involving longer sequences, noise, or unseen domains.
AI.method: CTC-driven teacher forcing: greedily decoded CTC pseudo-labels are fed into the decoder to generate attention targets in a single forward pass. The decoder predicts both CTC and CTC-driven attention pseudo-labels simultaneously (same length). Mixed sampling mitigates exposure bias of the decoder relying solely on CTC inputs.
AI.result: USR 2.0 halves training time, improves robustness to out-of-distribution inputs, and achieves state-of-the-art results on LRS3, LRS2, and WildVSR, surpassing USR and modality-specific self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: CTC-driven teacher forcing enables efficient and effective knowledge transfer despite potential global incoherence of pseudo-labels. The method benefits from CTC robustness and attention expressiveness without costly beam search, demonstrating that globally incoherent pseudo-labels are still valuable in semi-supervised settings.

title: A Statistical Approach for Modeling Irregular Multivariate Time Series with Missing Observations
comment: Accepted for publication in APSIPA Transactions on Signal and Information Processing
summary: Irregular multivariate time series with missing values present significant challenges for predictive modeling in domains such as healthcare. While deep learning approaches often focus on temporal interpolation or complex architectures to handle irregularities, we propose a simpler yet effective alternative: extracting time-agnostic summary statistics to eliminate the temporal axis. Our method computes four key features per variable-mean and standard deviation of observed values, as well as the mean and variability of changes between consecutive observations to create a fixed-dimensional representation. These features are then utilized with standard classifiers, such as logistic regression and XGBoost. Evaluated on four biomedical datasets (PhysioNet Challenge 2012, 2019, PAMAP2, and MIMIC-III), our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing recent transformer and graph-based models by 0.5-1.7% in AUROC/AUPRC and 1.1-1.7% in accuracy/F1-score, while reducing computational complexity. Ablation studies demonstrate that feature extraction-not classifier choice-drives performance gains, and our summary statistics outperform raw/imputed input in most benchmarks. In particular, we identify scenarios where missing patterns themselves encode predictive signals, as in sepsis prediction (PhysioNet, 2019), where missing indicators alone can achieve 94.2% AUROC with XGBoost, only 1.6% lower than using original raw data as input. Our results challenge the necessity of complex temporal modeling when task objectives permit time-agnostic representations, providing an efficient and interpretable solution for irregular time series classification.

AI.tldr: A simple yet effective method for irregular multivariate time series with missing values that extracts time-agnostic summary statistics (mean, standard deviation, and change statistics) per variable, then uses standard classifiers instead of complex deep learning architectures.
AI.motivation: Irregular multivariate time series with missing values present significant challenges for predictive modeling in healthcare. Existing deep learning approaches focus on temporal interpolation or complex architectures, but the authors propose a simpler yet effective alternative.
AI.method: Extract time-agnostic summary statistics per variable: mean and standard deviation of observed values, as well as the mean and variability of changes between consecutive observations. These features are used with standard classifiers (logistic regression, XGBoost) and evaluated on four biomedical datasets: PhysioNet Challenge 2012, 2019, PAMAP2, and MIMIC-III.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing recent transformer and graph-based models by 0.5-1.7% in AUROC/AUPRC and 1.1-1.7% in accuracy/F1-score while reducing computational complexity. Ablation studies show feature extraction drives performance gains, and summary statistics outperform raw/imputed input. In sepsis prediction, missing indicators alone achieve 94.2% AUROC with XGBoost.
AI.conclusion: The results challenge the necessity of complex temporal modeling when task objectives permit time-agnostic representations, providing an efficient and interpretable solution for irregular time series classification.

title: US-JEPA: A Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Medical Ultrasound
summary: Ultrasound (US) imaging poses unique challenges for representation learning due to its inherently noisy acquisition process. The low signal-to-noise ratio and stochastic speckle patterns hinder standard self-supervised learning methods relying on a pixel-level reconstruction objective. Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) address this drawback by predicting masked latent representations rather than raw pixels. However, standard approaches depend on hyperparameter-brittle and computationally expensive online teachers updated via exponential moving average. We propose US-JEPA, a self-supervised framework that adopts the Static-teacher Asymmetric Latent Training (SALT) objective. By using a frozen, domain-specific teacher to provide stable latent targets, US-JEPA decouples student-teacher optimization and pushes the student to expand upon the semantic priors of the teacher. In addition, we provide the first rigorous comparison of all publicly available state-of-the-art ultrasound foundation models on UltraBench, a public dataset benchmark spanning multiple organs and pathological conditions. Under linear probing for diverse classification tasks, US-JEPA achieves performance competitive with or superior to domain-specific and universal vision foundation model baselines. Our results demonstrate that masked latent prediction provides a stable and efficient path toward robust ultrasound representations.

AI.tldr: US-JEPA is a self-supervised ultrasound representation learning framework that uses a static teacher with the SALT objective to predict masked latent representations, achieving competitive or superior performance on diverse classification tasks compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound imaging faces unique challenges for representation learning due to low SNR and stochastic speckle patterns, which hinder standard self-supervised methods relying on pixel-level reconstruction. Standard Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) further depend on hyperparameter-brittle and computationally expensive online teachers updated via exponential moving average.
AI.method: US-JEPA adopts the Static-teacher Asymmetric Latent Training (SALT) objective, using a frozen, domain-specific teacher to provide stable latent targets. This decouples student-teacher optimization and pushes the student to expand upon the teacher's semantic priors through masked latent prediction rather than raw pixel reconstruction.
AI.result: On UltraBench (the first public benchmark comparing all publicly available state-of-the-art ultrasound foundation models), US-JEPA achieved performance competitive with or superior to both domain-specific and universal vision foundation model baselines under linear probing for diverse classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Masked latent prediction provides a stable and efficient path toward robust ultrasound representations, demonstrating the effectiveness of the SALT objective for ultrasound imaging tasks.

title: Grokking Finite-Dimensional Algebra
summary: This paper investigates the grokking phenomenon, which refers to the sudden transition from a long memorization to generalization observed during neural networks training, in the context of learning multiplication in finite-dimensional algebras (FDA). While prior work on grokking has focused mainly on group operations, we extend the analysis to more general algebraic structures, including non-associative, non-commutative, and non-unital algebras. We show that learning group operations is a special case of learning FDA, and that learning multiplication in FDA amounts to learning a bilinear product specified by the algebra's structure tensor. For algebras over the reals, we connect the learning problem to matrix factorization with an implicit low-rank bias, and for algebras over finite fields, we show that grokking emerges naturally as models must learn discrete representations of algebraic elements. This leads us to experimentally investigate the following core questions: (i) how do algebraic properties such as commutativity, associativity, and unitality influence both the emergence and timing of grokking, (ii) how structural properties of the structure tensor of the FDA, such as sparsity and rank, influence generalization, and (iii) to what extent generalization correlates with the model learning latent embeddings aligned with the algebra's representation. Our work provides a unified framework for grokking across algebraic structures and new insights into how mathematical structure governs neural network generalization dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper extends grokking analysis from group operations to more general finite-dimensional algebras (FDA), showing that learning multiplication amounts to learning a bilinear product specified by the structure tensor, and empirically investigating how algebraic properties and tensor structure influence grokking emergence and generalization.
AI.motivation: Prior grokking research focused primarily on group operations; this work extends analysis to more general algebraic structures including non-associative, non-commutative, and non-unital algebras to understand how mathematical structure governs neural network generalization.
AI.method: The paper connects group operation learning to FDA learning via structure tensors, showing learning multiplication equals learning a bilinear product. For real algebras, it connects to matrix factorization with implicit low-rank bias; for finite fields, it shows grokking emerges as models learn discrete representations.
AI.result: The authors experimentally investigate how algebraic properties (commutativity, associativity, unitality) influence grokking emergence and timing, how structure tensor properties (sparsity, rank) affect generalization, and the correlation between generalization and learning latent embeddings aligned with the algebra's representation.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified framework for grokking across algebraic structures, offering new insights into how mathematical structure governs neural network generalization dynamics.

title: DefenseSplat: Enhancing the Robustness of 3D Gaussian Splatting via Frequency-Aware Filtering
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for real-time and high-fidelity 3D reconstruction from posed images. However, recent studies reveal its vulnerability to adversarial corruptions in input views, where imperceptible yet consistent perturbations can drastically degrade rendering quality, increase training and rendering time, and inflate memory usage, even leading to server denial-of-service. In our work, to mitigate this issue, we begin by analyzing the distinct behaviors of adversarial perturbations in the low- and high-frequency components of input images using wavelet transforms. Based on this observation, we design a simple yet effective frequency-aware defense strategy that reconstructs training views by filtering high-frequency noise while preserving low-frequency content. This approach effectively suppresses adversarial artifacts while maintaining the authenticity of the original scene. Notably, it does not significantly impair training on clean data, achieving a desirable trade-off between robustness and performance on clean inputs. Through extensive experiments under a wide range of attack intensities on multiple benchmarks, we demonstrate that our method substantially enhances the robustness of 3DGS without access to clean ground-truth supervision. By highlighting and addressing the overlooked vulnerabilities of 3D Gaussian Splatting, our work paves the way for more robust and secure 3D reconstructions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a frequency-aware defense strategy for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that mitigates adversarial attacks by filtering high-frequency noise while preserving low-frequency content, achieving robustness without clean ground-truth supervision.
AI.motivation: Recent studies reveal 3DGS is vulnerable to adversarial corruptions in input views; imperceptible yet consistent perturbations can degrade rendering quality, increase training/rendering time, inflate memory usage, and even cause server denial-of-service, motivating a defense strategy to address these overlooked vulnerabilities.
AI.method: The authors analyze adversarial perturbations in low- and high-frequency components using wavelet transforms, then design a frequency-aware defense strategy that reconstructs training views by filtering high-frequency noise while preserving low-frequency content.
AI.result: The method effectively suppresses adversarial artifacts while maintaining scene authenticity, doesn't significantly impair training on clean data, and achieves a desirable trade-off between robustness and performance. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks under various attack intensities demonstrate substantial robustness improvement without needing clean ground-truth supervision.
AI.conclusion: By highlighting and addressing the overlooked vulnerabilities of 3D Gaussian Splatting, this work paves the way for more robust and secure 3D reconstructions, offering a simple yet effective defense mechanism.

title: The Sample Complexity of Replicable Realizable PAC Learning
summary:
In this paper, we consider the problem of replicable realizable PAC learning. We construct a particularly hard learning problem and show a sample complexity lower bound with a close to $(\log|H|)^{3/2}$ dependence on the size of the hypothesis class $H$. Our proof uses several novel techniques and works by defining a particular Cayley graph associated with $H$ and analyzing a suitable random walk on this graph by examining the spectral properties of its adjacency matrix.
  Furthermore, we show an almost matching upper bound for the lower bound instance, meaning if a stronger lower bound exists, one would have to consider a different instance of the problem.

AI.tldr: This paper studies replicable realizable PAC learning and establishes sample complexity lower bounds with $(\log|H|)^{3/2}$ dependence on the hypothesis class size, using novel techniques involving Cayley graphs and spectral analysis of random walks.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental problem of replicable realizable PAC learning, investigating the sample complexity required to learn hypothesis classes efficiently.
AI.method: The authors construct a particularly hard learning problem instance and employ novel techniques including: constructing a Cayley graph associated with the hypothesis class H, analyzing a random walk on this graph, and examining the spectral properties of its adjacency matrix.
AI.result: The paper proves a sample complexity lower bound with $(\log|H|)^{3/2}$ dependence on the hypothesis class size and shows an almost matching upper bound for this lower bound instance.
AI.conclusion: The results indicate that the $(\log|H|)^{3/2}$ bound is tight for this particular problem instance, suggesting that proving stronger lower bounds would require considering different learning problem instances.

title: RetinaVision: XAI-Driven Augmented Regulation for Precise Retinal Disease Classification using deep learning framework
summary: Early and accurate classification of retinal diseases is critical to counter vision loss and for guiding clinical management of retinal diseases. In this study, we proposed a deep learning method for retinal disease classification utilizing optical coherence tomography (OCT) images from the Retinal OCT Image Classification - C8 dataset (comprising 24,000 labeled images spanning eight conditions). Images were resized to 224x224 px and tested on convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures: Xception and InceptionV3. Data augmentation techniques (CutMix, MixUp) were employed to enhance model generalization. Additionally, we applied GradCAM and LIME for interpretability evaluation. We implemented this in a real-world scenario via our web application named RetinaVision. This study found that Xception was the most accurate network (95.25%), followed closely by InceptionV3 (94.82%). These results suggest that deep learning methods allow effective OCT retinal disease classification and highlight the importance of implementing accuracy and interpretability for clinical applications.
